topics.title: Tension Test on Mild Steel Using Universal Testing Machine and Extensometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL308 - CONTINUUM MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Tension Test on Mild Steel Using Universal Testing Machine and Extensometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Tension Test — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tension Test (Uniaxial Tensile Test):** A standardized destructive mechanical test in which a carefully prepared specimen of standard geometry is subjected to a gradually increasing uniaxial tensile load until fracture, while continuously recording the applied load and the corresponding extension, in order to evaluate fundamental mechanical properties of the material such as **Young's Modulus**, **Yield Strength**, **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)**, **Percentage Elongation**, and **Percentage Reduction in Area**.

The test is governed by Indian Standard **IS 1608 (Part 1):2018** (reaffirmed 2023) and its international equivalent **ASTM E8/E8M**. The KTU 2024 scheme laboratory manual mandates that mild steel (a ductile, low-carbon ferrous alloy) be tested as the canonical introductory material because its stress–strain response exhibits every classic feature (elastic region, upper/lower yield points, strain hardening, necking, and cup-and-cone fracture).

### 1.2 Universal Testing Machine (UTM) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Testing Machine (UTM):** A hydraulically or electromechanically actuated, computer- or dial-gauged test rig capable of applying controlled tensile, compressive, or flexural loads (commonly up to **1000 kN** capacity in academic laboratories) through interchangeable load cells, with a rigid two-column loading frame, adjustable crosshead, and wedge or threaded grips.

### 1.3 Extensometer — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extensometer:** A high-precision displacement transducer clamped axially to the reduced parallel section (gauge length) of the tensile specimen, used to measure minute elongations (typically with a resolution of **±0.001 mm** and a class better than **0.5%** as per **IS 1608 / ISO 9513**) without being affected by grip slippage, machine compliance, or crosshead slack.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are stretching a chewing-gum strip between your two hands. At first, the gum stretches a little and snaps back when you let go (this is the **elastic** region). If you pull harder, the gum stretches permanently and never goes back to its original length (this is the **plastic** region). Just before it breaks, it gets unusually thin in the middle — that thinning is called **necking**. The Tension Test is simply the *engineer's* version of this experiment, but done under a microscope: we know the exact force we are pulling with, the exact cross-section of the strip, and we measure the tiny stretching very accurately with a special caliper called an **extensometer**. Mild steel is the lab's "model citizen" because it shows *every* one of these stages very clearly on a graph.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mild Steel?** Mild steel (carbon content **0.15%–0.25%**) is chosen for the KTU lab because it is **ductile**, **inexpensive**, **readily available**, and its stress–strain curve contains all the classical features (linear elasticity, sharp yielding, strain hardening, necking, ductile fracture) required to teach every fundamental tensile property in a single test.

### 1.5 Why an Extensometer is Necessary (and not the crosshead alone)

The crosshead displacement of the UTM includes:
- Elastic stretch of the loading frame, grips, and load cell (machine compliance)
- Slippage at the grip–specimen interface
- Elastic stretch of the specimen shoulders (outside the gauge length)

These "pollute" the true material strain. An **extensometer** is mounted *only* on the parallel reduced section, so it captures **only the material's true deformation** — essential for accurate **Young's Modulus** and **yield strength** determination.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Engineering Logic — Step by Step

1. **Standardize the geometry** — a proportional or non-proportional round/flat specimen is machined as per IS 1608 so that test results from different labs are *comparable*.
2. **Measure original dimensions** — initial gauge length **L₀** and initial cross-sectional area **A₀** are precisely recorded.
3. **Mount in UTM** — the specimen is gripped axially, ensuring concentric loading (no bending).
4. **Attach extensometer** — to the parallel gauge length section, avoiding the shoulder fillets.
5. **Apply load monotonically** — quasi-static loading at a strain rate recommended by the standard (commonly **0.00025 /s to 0.0025 /s** within the elastic range).
6. **Record (Load, Extension) pairs** — synchronously from the load cell and the extensometer.
7. **Convert to (Stress, Strain)** — using the cross-section and gauge length.
8. **Identify the key points** — proportional limit, upper yield point, lower yield point, ultimate tensile strength, fracture point.
9. **Post-fracture measurements** — final gauge length **L_f** and final neck diameter **d_f** to compute ductility.
10. **Report as Engineering Stress vs Engineering Strain curve** (not True stress–strain, unless asked).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Standard geometry** removes geometric variables → *material-only* response.
- **Concentric loading** eliminates parasitic bending stresses that would falsely raise the apparent strength.
- **Extensometer on the gauge length** filters out machine and grip errors → *true* Young's modulus.
- **Monotonic quasi-static rate** ensures the test is **isothermal** and **strain-rate independent** for the elastic region.
- **Post-fracture measurements** are taken *carefully* (fractured pieces fitted back together) because the broken ends snap back elastically by a small amount.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Formula Cheat-Sheet — Tension Test on Mild Steel.** Memorize these; they appear in nearly every Part A and Part B answer.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $$\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A_0}$$ | P = applied tensile load; A₀ = original cross-sectional area | **N/mm² (MPa)** |
| 2 | Engineering Strain | $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$ | ΔL = change in gauge length; L₀ = original gauge length | **dimensionless** (or %) |
| 3 | Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity) | $$E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} \;\; \text{(in linear elastic region)} = \dfrac{P \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$$ | Slope of the initial straight-line portion of σ–ε curve | **GPa** (≈ 200 GPa for mild steel) |
| 4 | Proportional Limit | $\sigma_p$ | Stress up to which σ–ε curve obeys Hooke's law | MPa |
| 5 | Upper Yield Strength | $\sigma_{UYS}$ | First peak stress at which plastic deformation begins | MPa |
| 6 | Lower Yield Strength | $\sigma_{LYS}$ | Plateau stress during yielding (≈ used as **Yield Strength**) | MPa |
| 7 | Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) | $$\sigma_{UTS} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0}$$ | P_max = maximum load reached during the test | MPa |
| 8 | Breaking / Fracture Stress | $\sigma_f = P_f / A_0$ | Load at fracture | MPa |
| 9 | Percentage Elongation | $$\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$$ | L_f = final gauge length after fracture | **%** |
| 10 | Percentage Reduction in Area | $$\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$$ | A_f = final cross-sectional area at fracture (neck) | **%** |
| 11 | Proportional Gauge Length (Circular Section) | $$L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0} = 5 \cdot d_0$$ | For a round specimen, $L_0 = 5 d_0$ | mm |
| 12 | Proportional Gauge Length (Rectangular Section) | $$L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$$ | General proportional relation | mm |
| 13 | Resilience Modulus (Modulus of Resilience) | $$U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$ | Area under the elastic portion | **MPa** (J/m³) |
| 14 | Toughness Modulus | $U_t = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Total area under the σ–ε curve up to fracture | **MPa** (J/m³) |
| 15 | True Stress | $\sigma_T = P / A_i$ (at current area) | Differs from engineering stress after necking | MPa |
| 16 | True Strain | $\varepsilon_T = \ln(L_i / L_0)$ | Natural log of length ratio | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction — Engineering vs. True Stress/Strain.** Engineering stress uses the *original* area A₀ (used in KTU lab reports). True stress uses the *instantaneous* area (used in metal-forming theory). The KTU 2024 lab syllabus asks for **engineering** values, except for specific derivation questions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The tensile test data drive the **selection of materials** for every load-bearing structure:

- **Bridges, buildings, cranes:** require minimum yield strength for elastic design.
- **Pressure vessels, pipelines:** require minimum UTS for safety factor calculations.
- **Chains, cables, tow-bars:** require minimum UTS to resist peak loads.
- **Sheet-metal forming (car bodies):** require high %EL (formability).
- **Bolts, fasteners, rivets:** require both yield and UTS to prevent shear failure.

The numerical values obtained feed directly into design codes (IS 800, IS 2062, AISC, Eurocode 3) and FEA material libraries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proportional Gauge Length $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{A_0}$ (IS 1608)

For results from specimens of different cross-sections to be **comparable** in %EL, the gauge length must scale with the square root of the area. This is called the **proportional gauge length**, and the derivation is given below.

Let a circular specimen have original diameter **d₀** and gauge length **L₀**. Its original cross-sectional area is:

$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi \, d_0^{\,2}}{4}$$

Solving for the diameter:

$$d_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_0}{\pi}}$$

By the IS 1608 convention, the proportional gauge length for a round bar is fixed at:

$$L_0 = 5 \, d_0$$

Substituting the expression for **d₀** above:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0 &= 5 \, d_0 \\[6pt]
L_0 &= 5 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_0}{\pi}} \\[6pt]
L_0 &= \sqrt{\dfrac{25 \cdot 4 \, A_0}{\pi}} \\[6pt]
L_0 &= \sqrt{\dfrac{100 \, A_0}{\pi}} \\[6pt]
L_0 &= \sqrt{31.83 \, A_0} \\[6pt]
L_0 &\approx 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual Interpretation:** The constant **5.65** emerges from the geometric combination of the "5×diameter" rule and the area-of-a-circle formula, ensuring that the ratio $L_0 / \sqrt{A_0}$ is held *constant* across all round specimens so that %EL is geometry-independent. For a **rectangular** specimen of width **b** and thickness **t**, the same proportional rule applies directly, with $A_0 = b \cdot t$ giving $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{A_0}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Young's Modulus from a (Load, Extension) Record

The extensometer gives the change in gauge length **ΔL**, and the load cell gives the load **P**. To obtain **E**, we first convert to stress and strain.

The engineering stress:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A_0}$$

The engineering strain:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

By definition of Young's modulus in the linear elastic regime (Hooke's Law):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{P / A_0}{\Delta L / L_0} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{P \, L_0}{A_0 \, \Delta L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Practical Reading from the σ–ε Curve:** Compute the slope of the *initial straight line* by selecting two convenient points (σ₁, ε₁) and (σ₂, ε₂) and applying:

$$E = \dfrac{\sigma_2 - \sigma_1}{\varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1}$$

For mild steel the expected value lies in the range **190 GPa – 210 GPa**. Always quote E in **GPa**, not MPa, in your lab report.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — 0.2 % Offset Method for Yield Strength (used when no clear yield point exists)

Mild steel shows a sharp upper/lower yield point, but many other ductile materials (e.g., aluminium alloys) do not. The KTU 2024 syllabus demands knowledge of the **0.2 % offset method**, defined and derived below.

> **Definition:** The yield strength by the offset method is the stress at which the engineering stress–strain curve intersects a line drawn parallel to the initial elastic slope, but offset along the strain axis by an amount **0.002 (i.e., 0.2 %)**.

The equation of the offset line is:

$$\sigma_{offset} = E \cdot (\varepsilon - 0.002)$$

The yield point (σ_y) is the intersection of the offset line and the actual σ–ε curve, hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_y &= E \cdot (\varepsilon_y - 0.002) \\[4pt]
\text{where} \quad \varepsilon_y &= \varepsilon_{\text{at intersection}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Graphically (we will draw it in Section 4), draw a line starting at ε = 0.002 with slope **E**; the stress coordinate where this line first touches the actual curve is **σ_y**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Toughness (Area Under the σ–ε Curve)

Toughness is the *total* energy per unit volume a material can absorb before fracture, obtained by integration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_t &= \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon \\[6pt]
    &\approx \sum_{i} \tfrac{1}{2} (\sigma_{i+1} + \sigma_i)(\varepsilon_{i+1} - \varepsilon_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

In the KTU lab, the toughness is reported as the area under the engineering stress–strain curve, **estimated using Simpson's rule or the trapezoidal rule** on tabulated (σ, ε) data.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table for Specimen Sizing

| Standard | Specimen Type | Gauge Length Formula | Typical Use |
|----------|---------------|----------------------|--------------|
| **IS 1608 (Proportional)** | Round | $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0} = 5 d_0$ | KTU Lab default |
| **IS 1608 (Non-proportional)** | Round | $L_0 = 50 \,\text{mm}$ fixed | Sheet/strip |
| **ASTM E8 (Proportional)** | Round | $L_0 = 4 d_0$ | US equivalent |
| **ASTM E8 (Proportional)** | Flat | $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$ | Sheet |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Trap:** If the question gives only the **diameter** of a round specimen, you must compute $A_0 = \pi d_0^{\,2}/4$ *first*, then apply $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{A_0}$. Skipping the area computation is the #1 reason students lose marks in Part B.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Question:** A mild steel round specimen of diameter **d₀ = 12 mm** and gauge length **L₀ = 60 mm** yields under a load of **41.5 kN**. Compute (a) the yield strength, and (b) the corresponding strain at yield, taking **E = 200 GPa**.

**Part (a) — Yield Strength**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \dfrac{\pi \, (12)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 144}{4} = 113.10 \;\text{mm}^2 \\[6pt]
\sigma_y &= \dfrac{P_y}{A_0} = \dfrac{41\,500 \,\text{N}}{113.10 \,\text{mm}^2} = 366.93 \,\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Strain at Yield**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_y &= \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} = \dfrac{366.93 \,\text{MPa}}{200\,000 \,\text{MPa}} \\[6pt]
            &= 0.00183 \;\; \text{(dimensionless)} \\[4pt]
            &= 0.183 \,\text{%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This strain (~0.18 %) is very small, confirming that yielding of mild steel occurs at a barely-visible extension — a key reason extensometers (not the UTM crosshead) must be used.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the UTM Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hydraulic Power Pack] --> B[Servo Control Valve]
    B --> C[Loading Cylinder]
    C --> D[Crosshead Moving Platen]
    D --> E[Upper Wedge Grip]
    E --> F[Specimen with Extensometer]
    F --> G[Lower Wedge Grip]
    G --> H[Fixed Lower Platen]
    F --> I[Load Cell]
    I --> J[Data Acquisition System]
    F --> K[Extensometer]
    K --> J
    J --> L[Computer Display with Sigma vs Epsilon Plot]
    J --> M[Printer Output]
```

**Textual description of the block diagram (for the report):** The hydraulic power pack pressurises oil, which the servo valve regulates to drive the loading cylinder. The cylinder pushes the moving crosshead, which grips the specimen via the upper wedge grip. The reaction is taken through the lower grip, the fixed platen, and the load cell. Both the load-cell signal and the extensometer signal are fed to the data acquisition system (DAQ), which plots the engineering stress–strain curve in real time on the monitor and stores the data for post-processing.

### 4.2 Schematic of the Engineering Stress–Strain Curve for Mild Steel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    O[Origin] --> P[Region A: Linear Elastic Region]
    P --> P1[Proportional Limit sigma_p]
    P1 --> Q[Region B: Upper Yield Point sigma_UYS]
    Q --> R[Region C: Yield Plateau sigma_LYS]
    R --> S[Region D: Strain Hardening]
    S --> T[Region E: Ultimate Tensile Strength sigma_UTS]
    T --> U[Region F: Necking Localised]
    U --> V[Region G: Fracture sigma_f]
    V --> W[Point H: Final L_f and d_f for Ductility]
    P -.Hooke's Law slope equals E.-> P1
    S -.Strain hardening modulus.-> T
    T -.P_max divided by A_0.-> U
```

**Annotation of the regions (in the order they appear on the curve):**

| Region | Name | Key Property Read Off |
|--------|------|-----------------------|
| A | Linear elastic | Slope = **Young's Modulus E** |
| B | Upper yield point | First peak stress (mild steel) |
| C | Yield plateau | **Yield Strength σ_y** (average of plateau) |
| D | Strain hardening | Material gets stronger as it is plastically deformed |
| E | **Ultimate point** | **UTS = P_max / A₀** |
| F | Necking | Cross-section reduces locally |
| G | Fracture | **Breaking stress σ_f = P_f / A₀** |
| H | Post-fracture | Measure **L_f** and **d_f** for %EL and %RA |

### 4.3 Experimental Procedure Flowchart (KTU 2024 Lab Manual Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Receive and inspect specimen] --> S2[Step 2: Measure d0 at 3 places and average]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Mark gauge length L0 with a punch]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Mount specimen in UTM grips]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Attach extensometer on gauge length]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Set DAQ zero and start loading]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Apply load gradually and record data]
    S7 --> S8{Step 8: Has specimen fractured?}
    S8 -- No --> S7
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Step 9: Stop machine and remove fragments]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Fit fragments and measure L_f and d_f]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Convert load-extension to stress-strain]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12: Compute E, sigma_y, UTS, %EL, %RA]
    S12 --> S13[Step 13: Plot sigma vs epsilon curve]
    S13 --> S14[Step 14: Write the lab report]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram of a Typical Extensometer (Clip-On Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Specimen Gauge Section] --> B[Two Knife Edges Anchored to Specimen]
    B --> C[Flexible Cantilever Arm]
    C --> D[Strain Gauge or LVDT Element]
    D --> E[Amplifier and Filter]
    E --> F[DAQ Analog-to-Digital Channel]
    F --> G[PC Software for Strain Conversion]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme, May 2024]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *Define the Universal Testing Machine. Why is it called "universal"?*

**Model Answer:**
A Universal Testing Machine (UTM) is a hydraulically or electromechanically actuated test rig used to apply controlled tensile, compressive, or flexural loads to a specimen and measure the resulting deformation and load. It is called "universal" because, by changing the gripping fixtures and the loading direction, the *same* machine can perform multiple types of tests: tension, compression, bending (flexure), and sometimes shear and torsion. In a typical B.Tech lab, a 1000 kN capacity UTM is used, fitted with a load cell of appropriate capacity, an extensometer, and a computer-based data acquisition system.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme, Dec 2023]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *Why is an extensometer preferred over the UTM crosshead displacement for measuring strain in the elastic region?*

**Model Answer:**
The crosshead displacement of the UTM includes not only the elongation of the specimen's gauge length but also the elastic stretch of the loading frame, the load cell, the grips, and any slip at the grip–specimen interface (machine compliance). These extraneous contributions can be 5–10 times larger than the actual specimen strain in the elastic region, leading to grossly underestimated Young's Modulus. An extensometer, by contrast, is mounted directly on the parallel gauge section of the specimen, so it measures **only the true material deformation** with a resolution of ±0.001 mm and a class better than 0.5 % (IS 1608 / ISO 9513). Hence, an extensometer is essential for accurate determination of E and yield strength.

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model 2024 Scheme | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

---

#### Question A — Choice 1

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Universal Testing Machine used for the tension test on mild steel.*

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**
1. **Loading Frame:** A rigid two-column frame carries the upper (fixed) and lower (moving) crossheads, providing the reaction path for the applied tensile load.
2. **Hydraulic Power Pack:** An electric motor drives a pump that pressurises hydraulic oil, stored in a reservoir.
3. **Servo Control Valve:** The pressurised oil is metered into the loading cylinder by a servo/proportional valve, controlling the rate of crosshead motion.
4. **Loading Cylinder and Piston:** The pressurised oil pushes the piston, which is connected to the moving crosshead, applying a tensile force to the specimen.
5. **Gripping System:** Wedge-action or threaded grips grip the shouldered ends of the specimen. Proper alignment (concentric) avoids bending stresses.
6. **Load Cell:** Mounted in series with the specimen, the load cell is a calibrated strain-gauged element that produces an electrical signal proportional to the applied load.
7. **Extensometer:** A clip-on extensometer (strain-gauged or LVDT-based) is mounted on the gauge length to record the elongation.
8. **Data Acquisition System (DAQ) and Computer:** The load-cell and extensometer signals are digitised and plotted as a stress–strain curve on the monitor; the data are stored for post-processing.
9. **Safety Features:** Overload protection, emergency stop, and rupture guards protect the operator and machine.
10. **Operation:** Start the hydraulic pump, set the loading rate (≈ 0.5 mm/min for mild steel elastic range), clamp the specimen, attach the extensometer, zero the DAQ, and start loading. The σ–ε curve is plotted live until fracture.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A mild steel specimen of diameter **20 mm** and gauge length **60 mm** is tested in a UTM. The specimen yields at a load of **78.5 kN**, reaches a maximum load of **120 kN**, and fractures at **90 kN**. After fracture, the gauge length is **78 mm** and the neck diameter is **12 mm**. Compute (i) Yield Strength, (ii) Ultimate Tensile Strength, (iii) Percentage Elongation, and (iv) Percentage Reduction in Area.*

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

Initial cross-sectional area:

$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (20)^2}{4} = 314.16 \,\text{mm}^2$$

**(i) Yield Strength**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_y &= \dfrac{P_y}{A_0} = \dfrac{78\,500 \,\text{N}}{314.16 \,\text{mm}^2} \\[4pt]
        &= 249.87 \,\text{MPa} \;\approx\; 250 \,\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{UTS} &= \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \dfrac{120\,000 \,\text{N}}{314.16 \,\text{mm}^2} \\[4pt]
            &= 381.97 \,\text{MPa} \;\approx\; 382 \,\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage Elongation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%EL &= \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{78 - 60}{60} \times 100 \\[4pt]
     &= \dfrac{18}{60} \times 100 = 30 \,\text{%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Percentage Reduction in Area**

Final neck area:

$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (12)^2}{4} = 113.10 \,\text{mm}^2$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%RA &= \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{314.16 - 113.10}{314.16} \times 100 \\[4pt]
     &= \dfrac{201.06}{314.16} \times 100 = 64.0 \,\text{%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** This is a ductile mild steel with σ_y ≈ 250 MPa, UTS ≈ 382 MPa, %EL = 30 %, %RA = 64 %. The high %RA confirms the presence of significant necking before fracture.

---

#### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A)

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks]** *Explain with a sketch the stress–strain behaviour of mild steel in a tension test. Mark all key points and regions.*

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step with Region Identification):**

1. **Origin (O):** Zero stress, zero strain.
2. **Region A – Linear Elastic Region (O to A):** The stress–strain curve is a straight line. The slope of this line is **Young's Modulus E** (≈ 200 GPa for mild steel). The end of this region is the **proportional limit (σ_p)**.
3. **Region B – Upper Yield point (A to B):** The stress reaches a peak called the **upper yield strength (σ_UYS)** — the first point at which plastic deformation begins.
4. **Region C – Yield Plateau (B to C):** The stress drops to a lower value (σ_LYS) and remains nearly constant over a small strain range; this is the **lower yield strength**, and is reported as the **yield strength of the material (σ_y)**.
5. **Region D – Strain Hardening (C to D):** With continued plastic deformation, the material's resistance to deformation increases (dislocation entanglement), so the stress rises again.
6. **Region E – Ultimate Point (D):** The stress reaches its **maximum value σ_UTS** at the corresponding load **P_max**. Beyond this point, the engineering stress begins to fall, but only because of **localised necking**, not because the material is weakening.
7. **Region F – Necking (D to E):** The cross-section contracts locally; the true stress in the neck is still rising even though the engineering stress falls.
8. **Region G – Fracture (E):** The specimen finally breaks at the **fracture stress σ_f** with the characteristic **cup-and-cone** ductile fracture surface.
9. **Post-Fracture Measurements (F):** The two halves are fitted back together to measure **L_f** and **d_f**, used to compute **%EL** and **%RA**.

*Key labels on the sketch:* E (slope), σ_p, σ_UYS, σ_LYS (= σ_y), σ_UTS, σ_f, L_0, L_f, d_0, d_f.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *What is the 0.2 % offset method of determining yield strength? When is it used? Apply it to a hypothetical aluminium alloy whose σ–ε curve is linear up to 80 MPa with E = 70 GPa.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The 0.2 % offset method is a graphical procedure used to determine the **yield strength** of a material that does *not* exhibit a sharp yield point. A line is drawn parallel to the initial linear elastic portion of the σ–ε curve, but offset along the strain axis by an amount of **0.002 (i.e., 0.2 %)**. The stress at which this offset line intersects the σ–ε curve is defined as the **offset yield strength σ_0.2**.

**When it is used:** For materials that exhibit a **gradual transition from elastic to plastic behaviour** (no sharp yield point), e.g., aluminium alloys, copper, brass, stainless steel, and many polymers. Mild steel does *not* need this method (it has a clear yield plateau), but KTU 2024 includes it for conceptual completeness.

**Mathematical Formulation:** The equation of the offset line is:

$$\sigma_{offset} = E \cdot (\varepsilon - 0.002)$$

At the intersection point $(\varepsilon_y, \sigma_y)$:

$$\sigma_y = E \cdot (\varepsilon_y - 0.002)$$

**Numerical Application to the Hypothetical Aluminium Alloy:**

Given: **E = 70 GPa = 70 000 MPa**, linear-elastic limit = 80 MPa.

The 0.2 % offset line equation:

$$\sigma_{offset} = 70\,000 \cdot (\varepsilon - 0.002) = 70\,000\,\varepsilon - 140 \,\text{MPa}$$

Assume the actual plastic portion of the curve can be approximated by the line:

$$\sigma_{plastic} = 80 + 2\,000 \cdot (\varepsilon - \varepsilon_{p})$$

where $\varepsilon_p = 80 / 70\,000 = 0.00114$ (strain at the elastic limit).

Equating the two lines at the intersection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
70\,000\,\varepsilon - 140 &= 80 + 2\,000 \cdot (\varepsilon - 0.00114) \\
70\,000\,\varepsilon - 140 &= 80 + 2\,000\,\varepsilon - 2.28 \\
68\,000\,\varepsilon &= 217.72 \\
\varepsilon_y &= 0.00320
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\sigma_y = 70\,000 \cdot (0.00320 - 0.002) = 70\,000 \cdot 0.00120 = 84 \,\text{MPa}
$$

> **Reported yield strength:** **σ_0.2 ≈ 84 MPa** for the hypothetical aluminium alloy. In a real experiment, the intersection is read *graphically* from the plotted σ–ε curve, not solved algebraically.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Using A_f instead of A_0 in %RA** — %RA uses original A₀ in the *denominator*, not A_f. Reversing this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing %EL and %RA** — %EL is from the change in *length*; %RA is from the change in *area*. They measure two different aspects of ductility.
> 3. **Forgetting units for E** — KTU expects **GPa**, not MPa. A correct number in MPa loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Reporting engineering stress after necking as the "true" strength** — The KTU lab reports **engineering** values; flag this clearly in the report.
> 5. **Neglecting the extensometer class** — A 0.5 % or better class is required by IS 1608; mention this in the viva.
> 6. **Not plotting the σ–ε curve** — The graph itself is worth 3–4 marks. A neat, properly-labelled graph with axis units, key points marked, and a clear title is non-negotiable.
> 7. **Wrong gauge-length formula** — Use $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$ for *proportional* round specimens; use the *fixed* 50 mm gauge length for non-proportional specimens.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Tension test** is the most fundamental mechanical test; it gives **E, σ_y, UTS, %EL, %RA** from a single experiment.
- **UTM** stands for *Universal* Testing Machine — same machine does tension, compression, bending.
- **Extensometer** must be used for elastic-region strain measurement (not the crosshead).
- **IS 1608 / ASTM E8** govern specimen geometry; the proportional round specimen has $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0} = 5 d_0$.
- **Engineering stress** uses original area **A₀**; **engineering strain** uses original gauge length **L₀** — these are the KTU lab values.
- **Young's Modulus E** = slope of the initial linear portion of the σ–ε curve (≈ 200 GPa for mild steel).
- **Yield Strength** of mild steel = **lower yield point** (plateau value); for non-yielding materials, use the **0.2 % offset method**.
- **UTS = P_max / A₀** — note that this is the *engineering* UTS; true UTS would use the neck area.
- **%EL = (L_f − L₀)/L₀ × 100** — measured by fitting the fractured pieces back together.
- **%RA = (A₀ − A_f)/A₀ × 100** — A_f measured at the neck (smallest final diameter).
- **Ductile fracture** of mild steel produces a characteristic **cup-and-cone** surface.
- **Strain rate** must be quasi-static and within the IS 1608 envelope to be comparable with standard tabulated data.
- **Always report** Young's Modulus in **GPa**, stress in **MPa**, and gauge dimensions in **mm** — KTU examiners penalise inconsistent units.
- **Mild steel** carbon content = **0.15 % – 0.25 %**; it is the KTU lab's reference ductile material.
- **Typical mild-steel tensile values to memorise:** E ≈ 200 GPa, σ_y ≈ 250 MPa, UTS ≈ 400–500 MPa, %EL ≈ 20–30 %, %RA ≈ 50–65 %.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tests on Springs (Open and Close Coiled)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL308 - CONTINUUM MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Tests on Springs (Open and Close Coiled)

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Continuum Mechanics Lab (PCPEL308)**, the *Tests on Springs* experiment is a standard mechanical testing procedure designed to determine the **stiffness**, **deflection behavior**, and **strain energy storage capacity** of helical springs under axial tensile loading. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, students must experimentally evaluate both **Open Coiled (Extension/Tension) Helical Springs** and **Close Coiled (Compression) Helical Springs** to validate theoretical relationships derived from **Hooke's Law in torsion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A *helical spring* is a mechanical elastic element capable of storing mechanical energy by absorbing potential energy when subjected to an axial load. The stress induced in the spring wire is primarily **torsional shear stress**.

The two principal classifications tested in this experiment are:

| Spring Type | Common Name | Coils Touching? | Load Reaction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Open Coiled Spring** | Tension / Extension Spring | No (Small pitch) | Resists Tensile Pulling |
| **Close Coiled Spring** | Compression Spring | Yes (Adjacent coils in contact) | Resists Compressive Push |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Physical Intuition

Think of a **close-coiled spring** as a tightly wound Slinky toy that you push down on a table. Because the coils are touching each other, the spring instantly pushes back the moment you compress it, just like the shock absorbers in a car or motorcycle suspension.

On the other hand, imagine the same Slinky, but now its coils are slightly stretched apart with small gaps. This is an **open-coiled spring**. To use it, you must first pull it to close the gaps (this pull is called the *initial tension*). Only after the coils touch does it begin to act like a stiff spring, similar to the springs found in a clipboard, garage door mechanism, or weighing scale pointer return mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Memory Hook:** Open coiled = needs an **initial pull** to activate stiffness. Close coiled = stiffness is **immediate**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following engineering metrics are evaluated during the lab:

- **Wire Diameter (d)** — measured in **mm**
- **Mean Coil Diameter (D)** — measured in **mm**
- **Number of Active Coils (n)** — dimensionless integer
- **Spring Index (C)** — ratio `D/d` (typically 4 to 12)
- **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** — measured in **N/mm²** (for steel, G ≈ **80,000 N/mm²**)
- **Stiffness (k)** — measured in **N/mm**

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 viva voce, remember that **Open Coiled Springs** do not possess initial gap compression, hence the standard stiffness formula uses only the wire diameter and modulus of rigidity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Close Coiled (Compression) Helical Springs

When an axial compressive load **W** is applied to a close-coiled helical spring, the wire of the spring is subjected to a **twisting moment (torque)** and a **direct shear force**. Because the helix angle is very small (typically less than **10°**), the bending effect is considered negligible.

The torque generated on the wire cross-section is:
The axial load **W** acts at a distance equal to the mean radius **R** from the spring axis. This produces a torque **T = W × R**.

The shear stress induced in the wire is calculated using the **Maxwell's Reciprocity Theorem** (also known as the Equivalent Torsion Formula):

$$\tau = \frac{16WR}{\pi d^3} + \frac{4W}{\pi d^2}$$

For KTU exam purposes, the first term (torsional) is usually dominant, and the second term (direct shear) is often neglected in basic calculations, yielding:

$$\tau = \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$$

The deflection of a close-coiled spring under axial load is derived from Castigliano's theorem or strain energy principles. The total angular twist of the wire produces an axial deflection **δ**:

$$\delta = \frac{8WD^3 n}{G d^4}$$

The stiffness of the spring, denoted as **k**, is the ratio of load to deflection:

$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n}$$

## 2.2 Theory of Open Coiled (Extension/Tension) Helical Springs

An open-coiled spring differs fundamentally from a close-coiled spring because there is a gap between adjacent coils. When an axial tensile load **W** is applied, it must first overcome the **initial tension (Pᵢ)** before the spring begins to deflect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The deflection formula for an open-coiled spring contains an additional factor known as the **Beveridge Constant (q)** to account for the straightening of the helix angle and the initial gap closure.

The deflection equation for an open coiled spring is:

$$\delta = \frac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4} \times q$$

Where the **Beveridge Constant** is given by:

$$q = \frac{1}{1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^2 \times \left(\frac{1}{C_w}\right)}$$

And **C_w** is the Wahl's correction factor. For most KTU problems, a simpler approximation is used:

$$q \approx 1 + \frac{1}{2C^2}$$

The stiffness of an open coiled spring becomes:

$$k_{open} = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n \cdot q}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Close Coiled Spring | Open Coiled Spring |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Shear Stress (τ)** | $\dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$ | $\dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$ |
| **Deflection (δ)** | $\dfrac{8WD^3 n}{G d^4}$ | $\dfrac{8WD^3 n \cdot q}{G d^4}$ |
| **Stiffness (k)** | $\dfrac{G d^4}{8D^3 n}$ | $\dfrac{G d^4}{8D^3 n \cdot q}$ |
| **Strain Energy (U)** | $\dfrac{4W^2 D^3 n}{G d^4}$ | $\dfrac{4W^2 D^3 n \cdot q}{G d^4}$ |
| **Initial Tension (Pᵢ)** | Not applicable | Required to close gaps |
| **Units of k** | **N/mm** | **N/mm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Close-coiled springs are ubiquitous in vehicle suspensions, vibration dampers, and industrial die sets. Open-coiled springs are used in weighing balances, trampolines, surgical devices, and circuit breaker mechanisms where a preloaded tension is required.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Deflection for a Close Coiled Helical Spring

We begin by considering a small element of the spring wire. The strain energy **U** stored in a circular shaft of length **L** subjected to a torque **T** and a shear force **V** is given by the Castigliano's energy expression:

$$U = \int \frac{T^2}{2GJ} \, ds + \int \frac{V^2}{2GA} \, ds$$

For a helical spring of mean coil diameter **D**, wire diameter **d**, and **n** active coils, the total length of the wire is approximated as:

$$L = \pi D n$$

**Step 1:** Identify the internal forces. The axial load **W** acting on the spring produces:
- A torque on the wire: $T = W \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$
- A direct shear force on the wire: $V = W$

**Step 2:** Identify the geometric properties of the circular wire cross-section.
- Polar moment of inertia: $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$
- Cross-sectional area: $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$

**Step 3:** Substitute the torque expression into the strain energy integral for torsion:

$$U_{torsion} = \frac{(W \cdot D/2)^2}{2G \cdot (\pi d^4/32)} \cdot (\pi D n)$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the numerator and denominator:

$$U_{torsion} = \frac{W^2 D^2 / 4}{2G \cdot \pi d^4 / 32} \cdot \pi D n = \frac{W^2 D^2 \cdot 32}{8 G \pi d^4} \cdot \pi D n = \frac{4 W^2 D^3 n}{G d^4}$$

**Step 5:** Apply Castigliano's Second Theorem to find the deflection along the direction of **W**:

$$\delta = \frac{\partial U}{\partial W} = \frac{\partial}{\partial W}\left(\frac{4 W^2 D^3 n}{G d^4}\right) = \frac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4}$$

**Step 6:** Derive the stiffness:

$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Beveridge Constant for Open Coiled Spring

An open-coiled spring stores additional strain energy due to the **bending moment** acting on the wire (caused by the helix angle) and the **axial elongation of the wire itself**.

**Step 1:** Write the modified strain energy expression including the bending energy term $U_{bending}$:

$$U_{total} = \frac{4 W^2 D^3 n}{G d^4} + \frac{W^2 D^3 n}{2 E I}$$

**Step 2:** Introduce the flexural rigidity $EI$ in terms of $GJ$. Using the relationship $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ and $I = J/2$, we obtain:

$$U_{bending} = \frac{W^2 D^3 n \cdot 2(1+\nu)}{G d^4}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Castigliano's theorem to the combined strain energy:

$$\delta = \frac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4} + \frac{4 W D^3 n (1+\nu)}{G d^4} = \frac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4} \left( 1 + \frac{(1+\nu)}{2} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Group the bracketed terms into the **Beveridge Constant q**:

$$q = 1 + \frac{(1+\nu)}{2}$$

For a Poisson's ratio of **0.3** (typical for steel), this gives $q \approx 1.15$, which is why open-coiled springs deflect **slightly more** than close-coiled springs under the same load.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Spring Testing Rig

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of the **Universal Spring Testing Machine** as used in the PCPEL308 laboratory. This represents the complete interaction between the specimen, the loading mechanism, and the data acquisition system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lab Power ON and Rig Calibration] --> B[Measure Wire Diameter d with Screw Gauge]
    B --> C[Measure Mean Coil Diameter D with Vernier Caliper]
    C --> D[Count Active Coils n]
    D --> E[Mount Spring on Hanging Bracket]
    E --> F{Is Spring Open or Close Coiled?}
    F -->|Close Coiled| G[Place on Compression Platform]
    F -->|Open Coiled| H[Attach to Tension Hooks]
    G --> I[Set Dial Gauge Zero on Top Plate]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Apply Incremental Load W on Pan]
    J --> K[Record Deflection delta from Dial Gauge]
    K --> L{More Load Increments?}
    L -->|Yes| J
    L -->|No| M[Plot Load vs Deflection Graph]
    M --> N[Determine Slope k Experimental]
    N --> O[Compare with Theoretical k from Formula]
    O --> P[Calculate Percentage Error]
    P --> Q[Result and Viva voce Documentation]
```

## 4.2 Stress Distribution Schematic on Spring Wire Cross-Section

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **internal stress distribution** experienced by the wire cross-section when the spring is under axial load. This is critical for understanding the **Wahl's Factor** application.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Applied Axial Load W] --> M[Torque T on Wire = W times D/2]
    L --> N[Direct Shear Force V = W]
    M --> O[Torsional Shear Stress component]
    N --> P[Transverse Shear Stress component]
    O --> Q[Maximum Shear Stress at Outer Fibers]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Combined Stress State at Surface of Wire]
    R --> S[Spring Deflection delta and Energy Storage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The torque **T** creates the dominant torsional shear stress that reaches its maximum value at the outermost fibers of the wire (i.e., at radius **d/2**). The direct shear **V** adds a secondary stress component that becomes significant only for springs with a very small spring index **C < 4**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between an open coiled helical spring and a close coiled helical spring in terms of coil contact and load application.**

**Model Answer:** A **close coiled helical spring** has its adjacent coils in contact with each other with negligible pitch, and it is designed to resist compressive loads. An **open coiled helical spring** has a small gap between its adjacent coils and must overcome an **initial tension force** before it begins to deflect under a tensile load. Close coiled springs are used in compression applications, whereas open coiled springs are used in tension applications such as in weighing scales and trampolines. **(3 Marks)**

### Question 2: Formula Recall `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State the expression for the stiffness of a close coiled helical spring and define each term.**

**Model Answer:** The stiffness of a close coiled helical spring is given by the formula:

$$k = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n}$$

Where **G** is the Modulus of Rigidity of the wire material in N/mm², **d** is the wire diameter in mm, **D** is the mean coil diameter in mm, and **n** is the number of active coils. The unit of stiffness is **N/mm**. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Derivation and Application `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the deflection of a close coiled helical spring subjected to an axial load W. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Consider a small element of the spring wire. The applied load **W** at a mean radius **R = D/2** creates a torque **T = WR** and a direct shear force **V = W**.

**Step 2:** The strain energy stored in the spring wire of length **L = πDn** is:

$$U = \frac{T^2 L}{2GJ} + \frac{V^2 L}{2GA}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the geometric properties $J = \pi d^4/32$ and $A = \pi d^2/4$:

$$U = \frac{(W \cdot D/2)^2 \cdot \pi D n}{2G \cdot (\pi d^4/32)} = \frac{4 W^2 D^3 n}{G d^4}$$

**Step 4:** Applying Castigliano's Theorem $\delta = \partial U / \partial W$:

$$\delta = \frac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4}$$

**(7 Marks — Awarded for correct identification of torque, strain energy setup, algebraic simplification, and final Castigliano application)**

**(b) A close coiled helical spring has a wire diameter of 6 mm, a mean coil diameter of 60 mm, and 10 active coils. It is made of steel with a Modulus of Rigidity of 80,000 N/mm². Determine the stiffness and the deflection under an axial load of 200 N. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the given parameters.
- Wire diameter: $d = 6$ mm
- Mean coil diameter: $D = 60$ mm
- Number of active coils: $n = 10$
- Modulus of Rigidity: $G = 80{,}000$ N/mm²
- Applied load: $W = 200$ N

**Step 2:** Calculate the stiffness using the formula:

$$k = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 6^4}{8 \times 60^3 \times 10}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator $80{,}000 \times 1296 = 103{,}680{,}000$.

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator $8 \times 216{,}000 \times 10 = 17{,}280{,}000$.

**Step 5:** Final stiffness calculation:

$$k = \frac{103{,}680{,}000}{17{,}280{,}000} = 6.0 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the deflection:

$$\delta = \frac{W}{k} = \frac{200}{6.0} = 33.33 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** Stiffness = **6.0 N/mm** and Deflection = **33.33 mm** **(7 Marks — Awarded for correct substitution, accurate arithmetic, and final boxed answer)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`

**(a) Explain the concept of the Beveridge Constant for an open coiled helical spring. Why is its value greater than unity? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Definition: The **Beveridge Constant (q)** is a dimensionless multiplier used in the deflection formula of an open coiled helical spring to account for the additional deflection caused by the **bending of the wire** and the **axial elongation of the wire** due to the helical curvature.

**Step 2:** Mathematical expression: $q = 1 + \dfrac{(1+\nu)}{2}$ where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio.

**Step 3:** Reason for q > 1: In a close coiled spring, the helix angle is small and the wire is assumed to be under pure torsion. However, in an open coiled spring, the larger pitch creates a measurable helix angle, and the axial load induces a bending moment on the wire in addition to the torque. This additional bending causes extra deflection, making the actual deflection larger than that predicted by the pure torsion formula alone.

**Step 4:** Typical value: For steel with $\nu = 0.3$, the Beveridge Constant evaluates to $q = 1.15$, meaning the actual deflection is approximately **15% greater** than the close-coiled prediction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) An open coiled helical spring is made of steel wire of 2 mm diameter, coiled to a mean diameter of 20 mm with 15 active coils. The Modulus of Rigidity is 80,000 N/mm² and the Poisson's ratio is 0.3. Find the stiffness of the spring. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Given data extraction.
- $d = 2$ mm, $D = 20$ mm, $n = 15$, $G = 80{,}000$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.3$

**Step 2:** Calculate the Beveridge Constant:

$$q = 1 + \frac{(1 + 0.3)}{2} = 1 + 0.65 = 1.65$$

**Step 3:** Apply the open coiled stiffness formula:

$$k_{open} = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n \cdot q} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 2^4}{8 \times 20^3 \times 15 \times 1.65}$$

**Step 4:** Numerator: $80{,}000 \times 16 = 1{,}280{,}000$.

**Step 5:** Denominator: $8 \times 8000 \times 15 \times 1.65 = 1{,}584{,}000$.

**Step 6:** Final stiffness:

$$k_{open} = \frac{1{,}280{,}000}{1{,}584{,}000} = 0.808 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Result:** Stiffness of open coiled spring = **0.808 N/mm** **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Beveridge Constant:** The most common error in KTU valuation is applying the close-coiled formula directly to an open-coiled spring. This results in a **2 to 3 mark deduction**.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students frequently mix up cm and mm. Always convert all linear dimensions to **mm** and **G** to **N/mm²** before substitution.
> 3. **Counting Total Coils Instead of Active Coils:** For springs with end hooks or loops, subtract the inactive end coils (typically 1 to 2 coils) from the total count.
> 4. **Ignoring the Direct Shear Term:** In the shear stress formula, neglecting the $\dfrac{4W}{\pi d^2}$ term is acceptable for standard problems, but a partial mark is awarded if students correctly state that this term has been neglected.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

- **Open vs Close Coiled:** Open coiled springs have gaps and resist tension; close coiled springs have touching coils and resist compression.
- **Core Formula for Stiffness:** $k = \dfrac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n}$ for close coiled and includes a factor **q** for open coiled.
- **Beveridge Constant (q):** Always greater than 1, typically **1.15** for steel with $\nu = 0.3$.
- **Spring Index C = D/d:** Should lie between **4 and 12** for a stable, manufacturable spring.
- **Modulus of Rigidity for Steel:** Standard value to remember is **G = 80,000 N/mm²** (or 80 GPa).
- **Castigliano's Theorem:** The fundamental energy method used to derive the deflection of helical springs.
- **Strain Energy Storage:** A spring is a mechanical energy storage device — the area under the Load vs Deflection graph represents the strain energy.
- **Lab Procedure:** Always measure **d** with a screw gauge and **D** with a Vernier caliper for maximum accuracy in KTU lab evaluations.
- **Wahl's Factor:** A correction factor for stress concentration at the inner fibers; not always required but good to mention in viva voce for higher marks.
- **Direct Shear Term:** The $\dfrac{4W}{\pi d^2}$ term is often neglected in basic KTU problems but should be acknowledged in the derivation for full marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Revision Strategy:** Memorize the four master equations (stress, deflection, stiffness, strain energy) for both spring types. Be ready to derive at least one of them from Castigliano's theorem during the viva voce, as this is a **guaranteed 5-mark question** in KTU 2024 Scheme lab examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardness Test (Brinell, Vickers and Rockwell)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL308 - CONTINUUM MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Hardness Test (Brinell, Vickers and Rockwell)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Hardness (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hardness** is formally defined as the **resistance of a material to localized plastic (permanent) deformation** caused by the mechanical indentation of a harder indenter, or by mechanical abrasion, or by scratching.

In the context of **PCPEL308 – Continuum Mechanics Lab**, hardness testing falls under the broader umbrella of **mechanical characterization of materials** and is a key **destructive/non-destructive** evaluation technique. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** nomenclature adopted from ASTM E10, E18, and E92 standards:

- **Brinell Hardness (HBW / HBS)** — Macro-hardness, ball indenter.
- **Vickers Hardness (HV)** — Micro/Macro-hardness, diamond pyramid indenter.
- **Rockwell Hardness (HR)** — Direct depth measurement, multiple scales.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine pressing a thumb tack (indenter) into a soft eraser versus a block of wood. The wood resists much more than the eraser. Hardness is a numerical measure of "how strongly" the material pushes back against the indenter trying to sink in.

- A **soft material** → indentation is **large** → hardness number is **low**.
- A **hard material** → indentation is **small** → hardness number is **high**.

### Real-World Analogy Table

| Scenario | Indenter | Material Tested | Outcome |
|----------|----------|-----------------|---------|
| Pressing a ball into butter | Steel ball | Butter (soft) | Large dent, low HB |
| Pressing a diamond tip on steel | Diamond cone | Hardened steel | Tiny dent, high HRC |
| Walking on sand with heel | Heel | Sand beach | Heel sinks easily |

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **three fundamental quantities** measured in any hardness test are:
> 1. **Applied Load (F)** — measured in **kgf** (kilogram-force) or **N** (Newton).
> 2. **Indenter Geometry** — ball diameter, pyramid angle, or cone angle.
> 3. **Indentation Size** — diameter (d), diagonal length (d), or depth (h).

**Conversion Constant:** $1 \text{ kgf} = 9.80665 \text{ N}$

**Standard Indenter Angles for Vickers:** The square diamond pyramid has an angle of **136°** between opposite faces — this was chosen historically so that Vickers hardness numbers roughly match Brinell numbers up to ~300 HB.

**Rockwell Standard Scale Identifier (Partial List):**
- **HRC** — Diamond cone, 150 kgf (most common for steels).
- **HRB** — 1/16" steel ball, 100 kgf (brass, aluminium).
- **HRA** — Diamond cone, 60 kgf (very hard materials like cemented carbides).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Hardness Testing

The operational logic of indentation-based hardness testing is rooted in the **continuum mechanics principle of plastic flow**. When a hard indenter is pressed into a ductile material under a known load:

1. **Elastic deformation** occurs first (recoverable).
2. Upon exceeding the **yield strength**, **plastic deformation** begins.
3. A permanent impression forms whose geometry encodes the material's resistance.
4. The hardness is then mathematically back-calculated from this geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> Hardness is **not a fundamental material property** (unlike Young's modulus or density). It is an *engineered empirical index* — meaning its value depends on the indenter, the load, and the test method used. Direct comparison between HB, HV, and HR is **approximate** and requires standard conversion charts.

## 2.2 Brinell Hardness Test — Operational Logic

### Step-by-Step Procedural Logic
- A **hardened steel ball (HBS)** or **tungsten carbide ball (HBW)** of diameter $D$ is forced under a known load $F$ into the specimen surface.
- The load is held for a **dwell time** (typically **10–15 s** for steels, **30 s** for non-ferrous metals).
- After removal of load, the **diameter $d$** of the circular indentation is measured (using a low-power microscope with a graduated scale).
- The **Brinell Hardness Number (BHN)** is computed.

### Standard Load-Diameter Combinations (ASTM E10)
- For **steels and cast irons**: $F = 3000 \text{ kgf}$, $D = 10 \text{ mm}$ → $F/D^2 = 30$.
- For **copper and its alloys**: $F = 1000 \text{ kgf}$, $D = 10 \text{ mm}$ → $F/D^2 = 10$.
- For **aluminium and soft metals**: $F = 500 \text{ kgf}$, $D = 10 \text{ mm}$ → $F/D^2 = 5$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **$F/D^2$ ratio must remain constant** for results to be comparable across different specimen sizes and load levels. This is the foundation of the "geometrically similar indentation" principle in Brinell testing.

### Why Use a Ball Indenter?
A ball produces a relatively **large, well-defined impression** even on coarse-grained or heterogeneous materials, making it ideal for **castings and forgings**. However, for very hard materials (>450 HB), the ball itself deforms, leading to errors — hence the Brinell test is restricted to **softer materials** in practice.

## 2.3 Vickers Hardness Test — Operational Logic

### Step-by-Step Procedural Logic
- A **square-based diamond pyramid indenter** with a face angle of **136°** is pressed into the specimen.
- Standard loads: **1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 100, 120 kgf** (any of these).
- After dwell time (~10–15 s), the **two diagonals $d_1$ and $d_2$** of the square indentation are measured.
- The **average diagonal $d = (d_1 + d_2)/2$** is used for calculation.
- The **Vickers Hardness Number (VHN or HV)** is computed.

### Why Use a Diamond Pyramid?
- Diamond is the **hardest known material** (Mohs 10), so the indenter does not deform even at high loads.
- The square impression is **geometrically similar at all loads** — meaning HV is **load-independent** (a major advantage over Brinell).
- Suitable for an **enormous range** from soft aluminium (~5 HV) to hardened tool steel (~1000 HV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **136° face angle** was specifically chosen because $\tan(68°) \approx 2.5$, which makes the Vickers hardness number **numerically equivalent to the Brinell hardness number** up to approximately 300 HB. Above 300, the Brinell ball flattens and HBW increases more slowly than HV.

## 2.4 Rockwell Hardness Test — Operational Logic

### The "Two-Load" Procedure (Unique to Rockwell)
- **Minor load (preliminary load):** $F_0 = 10 \text{ kgf}$ is applied first to seat the specimen and eliminate surface irregularities.
- The **reference depth $h_0$** (after minor load) is set to zero on the dial gauge.
- **Major load $F_1$** is then applied, causing additional indentation depth $h_1$.
- The major load is **removed** (but minor load retained), and the elastic recovery brings the depth to $h$.
- The **permanent penetration $e = h - h_0$** is measured.

### Why a Two-Load System?
This clever procedure **eliminates errors** from:
- Surface roughness
- Specimen seating
- Elastic deformation
- Minor frame compliance

> [!NOTE]
> The Rockwell number is read **directly from the dial** — no optical measurement of indentation size is needed, making it the **fastest** of the three tests and ideal for production-line quality control.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Test** | **Indenter** | **Load** | **Measured Quantity** | **Hardness Formula** | **Units** |
|----------|--------------|----------|------------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| Brinell (HB) | Steel / WC ball, diameter $D$ | $F$ (kgf) | Indentation diameter $d$ (mm) | $HB = \dfrac{2F}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}$ | kgf/mm² |
| Brinell (Alt.) | Same as above | $F$ | Depth $h$ (mm) | $HB = \dfrac{F}{\pi D h}$ | kgf/mm² |
| Vickers (HV) | Diamond pyramid, 136° face angle | $F$ (kgf) | Mean diagonal $d$ (mm) | $HV = \dfrac{1.8544 \cdot F}{d^2}$ | kgf/mm² |
| Rockwell (C) | Diamond cone, 120° | 150 kgf | Depth difference $e$ | $HRC = 100 - \dfrac{e}{0.002}$ | dimensionless |
| Rockwell (B) | 1/16" steel ball | 100 kgf | Depth difference $e$ | $HRB = 130 - \dfrac{e}{0.001}$ | dimensionless |
| Rockwell (A) | Diamond cone, 120° | 60 kgf | Depth difference $e$ | $HRA = 100 - \dfrac{e}{0.002}$ | dimensionless |
| General Rockwell (Diamond) | Diamond cone | $F_1$ | Depth $e$ | $HR = N - \dfrac{e}{0.002}$ | dimensionless |
| General Rockwell (Ball) | Steel ball | $F_1$ | Depth $e$ | $HR = N - \dfrac{e}{0.001}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the formulas above, $N$ is a **constant chosen to give a positive scale starting at zero**. For diamond indenters $N = 100$; for ball indenters $N = 130$. The number $1.8544$ in the Vickers formula equals $2 \sin(136°/2) = 2 \sin(68°)$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Industry Application** | **Preferred Test** | **Reason** |
|--------------------------|---------------------|------------|
| Heat-treated steel components (gears, shafts) | Rockwell C (HRC) | Fast, direct reading, production-friendly |
| Surface-hardened case (e.g., induction hardened) | Rockwell Superficial (e.g., 15N) | Low load avoids penetrating the case |
| Hardness of copper busbars, brass fittings | Brinell or Rockwell B | Soft, large grain — Brinell averages well |
| Research / small samples / thin coatings | Vickers / Micro-Vickers | Geometrical similarity, wide range |
| Quality control in mass production (automotive) | Rockwell | Fastest, automated, minimal operator skill |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Brinell Hardness Formula

### Starting Concept
Brinell hardness is defined as the **ratio of the applied load to the curved surface area of the indentation** (not the projected area). This is a historical convention.

The curved surface area $A_s$ of a spherical cap indentation of depth $h$ on a sphere of diameter $D$ is:

$$A_s = \pi D h$$

### Step 1: Express $h$ in terms of the measured diameter $d$
The relationship between indentation depth $h$, ball diameter $D$, and measured indentation diameter $d$ is derived from the geometry of intersecting circles (Pythagorean theorem on the right triangle formed by ball radius, half-chord, and depth).

From the geometry:

$$\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{D}{2} - h\right)^2 + \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2$$

Expanding the squared term:

$$\frac{D^2}{4} = \frac{D^2}{4} - D h + h^2 + \frac{d^2}{4}$$

Subtracting $\frac{D^2}{4}$ from both sides:

$$0 = -D h + h^2 + \frac{d^2}{4}$$

Solving for $h$ (ignoring the trivial $h = 0$ solution using the quadratic formula, or using the small-$h$ approximation):

For **shallow indentations** ($h \ll D$), the $h^2$ term is negligible compared to $D h$, giving:

$$h = \frac{d^2}{2D}$$

This is the **standard Brinell approximation** and is universally used in practice because $d$ is typically 0.2 to 0.6 times $D$.

### Step 2: Substitute into the surface area expression
$$A_s = \pi D h = \pi D \cdot \frac{d^2}{2D} = \frac{\pi d^2}{2}$$

This is the surface area using the approximation. For the **exact** expression without approximation:

$$A_s = \pi D h = \frac{\pi D}{2}\left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)$$

### Step 3: Define Brinell Hardness as Load per Unit Curved Area
$$HB = \frac{F}{A_s}$$

**Exact formula:**

$$HB = \frac{2F}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}$$

**Approximate formula** (using $h = d^2/2D$):

$$HB \approx \frac{2F}{\pi d^2}$$

### Worked Numerical Example — Brinell Test

**Given:** $F = 3000 \text{ kgf}$, $D = 10 \text{ mm}$, $d = 4.5 \text{ mm}$ (measured diameter of indentation).

**Solution:**

Compute the term inside the square root:

$$D^2 - d^2 = 10^2 - 4.5^2 = 100 - 20.25 = 79.75 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\sqrt{79.75} = 8.9300 \text{ mm}$$

Compute the bracket term:

$$D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} = 10 - 8.9300 = 1.0700 \text{ mm}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\pi D \cdot (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}) = \pi \times 10 \times 1.0700 = 33.608 \text{ mm}^2$$

Apply the Brinell formula:

$$HB = \frac{2 \times 3000}{33.608} = \frac{6000}{33.608} = 178.5 \text{ kgf/mm}^2$$

**Result:** $HB \approx 178.5$, reported as **HBW 178.5** (tungsten carbide ball) or **HBS 178.5** (steel ball).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Vickers Hardness Formula

### Starting Concept
Vickers hardness is defined as the **load divided by the projected (contact) area of the indentation**, unlike Brinell which uses curved area.

### Step 1: Geometry of the Square Pyramid Impression
Let $d$ be the mean diagonal of the square impression and $\theta = 136°$ the angle between opposite faces of the pyramid.

The half-diagonal of the square base is $d/2$. The slant height of the pyramid (from tip to a corner of the base) makes an angle of $90° - \theta/2 = 22°$ with the base.

The **depth of indentation** $h$ is:

$$h = \frac{d}{2} \tan\left(\frac{180° - 136°}{2}\right) = \frac{d}{2} \tan(22°)$$

### Step 2: Projected Contact Area
The projected area of a square of diagonal $d$ is:

$$A_p = d^2 / 2$$

The side of the square is $a = d/\sqrt{2}$, so:

$$A_p = a^2 = (d/\sqrt{2})^2 = d^2/2$$

### Step 3: Vickers Hardness Definition
$$HV = \frac{F}{A_p} = \frac{F}{d^2/2} = \frac{2F}{d^2}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Constant
Computing the exact constant requires the projected area in terms of $F$ and the surface area of the pyramid (alternate definition used by Vickers):

$$A_s = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(\theta/2)} = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(68°)} = \frac{d^2}{1.8544}$$

The classical Vickers formula uses the **surface area** convention (similar to Brinell):

$$HV = \frac{F}{A_s} = \frac{2F \sin(\theta/2)}{d^2} = \frac{1.8544 \cdot F}{d^2}$$

where $1.8544 = 2 \sin(68°)$.

### Worked Numerical Example — Vickers Test

**Given:** $F = 30 \text{ kgf}$, $d_1 = 0.420 \text{ mm}$, $d_2 = 0.430 \text{ mm}$.

**Solution:**

Mean diagonal:

$$d = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{0.420 + 0.430}{2} = 0.425 \text{ mm}$$

Square the diagonal:

$$d^2 = (0.425)^2 = 0.180625 \text{ mm}^2$$

Apply Vickers formula:

$$HV = \frac{1.8544 \times 30}{0.180625} = \frac{55.632}{0.180625} \approx 307.96 \text{ kgf/mm}^2$$

**Result:** $HV \approx 308$, reported as **HV 308** at 30 kgf load.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Rockwell Hardness Number

### Starting Concept
The Rockwell number is defined such that:
- Each **0.002 mm of permanent penetration** beyond the reference position corresponds to **1 unit of Rockwell hardness** (for diamond indenters).
- For ball indenters, the divisor is **0.001 mm** per unit.

### Step 1: General Definition
Let $e$ be the **net permanent depth** of indentation after removal of the major load (but with minor load still applied). The Rockwell number is:

$$HR = N - \frac{e}{k}$$

where $N$ is a scale constant and $k$ is the unit depth (0.002 mm for diamond, 0.001 mm for ball).

### Step 2: Derivation of Scale Constants $N$ and $k$

**For HRC (Diamond cone, 150 kgf major load):**
- The diamond indenter, under major load, can produce a maximum depth of $e_{max} = 0.200 \text{ mm}$ (this is a defined geometric limit for the standard diamond indenter).
- We require the scale to give $HR = 0$ at this maximum depth.
- Setting $HR = 0$ at $e = 0.200$ mm and using $k = 0.002$ mm/unit:
- $0 = N - 0.200/0.002 = N - 100 \Rightarrow N = 100$
- Therefore: $HRC = 100 - e/0.002$

**For HRB (1/16" ball, 100 kgf major load):**
- For ball indenters, $k = 0.001$ mm/unit.
- A maximum depth of $e_{max} = 0.130$ mm gives $HR = 0$:
- $0 = N - 0.130/0.001 = N - 130 \Rightarrow N = 130$
- Therefore: $HRB = 130 - e/0.001$

### Step 3: Sign Convention Clarification
A **larger** penetration $e$ gives a **smaller** hardness number. The minus sign is intentional and reflects that the dial gauge reads in the "compressive direction" by convention.

### Worked Numerical Example — Rockwell C Test

**Given:** Net permanent depth after major load removal: $e = 0.085 \text{ mm}$.

**Solution:**

Apply the HRC formula:

$$HRC = 100 - \frac{0.085}{0.002} = 100 - 42.5 = 57.5$$

**Result:** $HRC = 57.5$, indicating a **medium-hardened tool steel** (typical range for HRC 55–62 is quench-hardened bearing steel or high-carbon tool steel).

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| **Feature** | **Brinell (HB)** | **Vickers (HV)** | **Rockwell (HR)** |
|-------------|------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Indenter type | Steel/WC ball | Diamond pyramid | Diamond cone or ball |
| Indenter size | $D = 1, 2, 2.5, 5, 10$ mm | Single (geometrically similar) | Ball: 1/16", 1/8", 1/4", 1/2"; Cone: standard |
| Load | 1–3000 kgf | 1–120 kgf | 15–150 kgf major |
| Measured quantity | Indentation diameter $d$ | Diagonal $d$ | Depth $e$ |
| Reading method | Optical (microscope) | Optical (microscope) | Direct dial gauge |
| Time per test | 1–2 min | 1–2 min | 5–10 s |
| Best for | Soft-medium, coarse grain | Wide range, research | Production QC, fast testing |
| Range of validity | HB 1–450 | HV 5–2000 | HR depends on scale |
| Major advantage | Simple, robust | Geometrically similar at all loads | Fastest, no optical measurement |
| Major limitation | Ball flattens at high hardness | Requires surface polish | Scale-dependent, many scales |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Brinell Test Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Prepare Specimen Surface] --> B[Mount Specimen on Anvil]
    B --> C[Apply Minor Pre-load = 0]
    C --> D[Apply Major Load F via Lever System]
    D --> E[Dwell Time: 10-30 s]
    E --> F[Remove Load Slowly]
    F --> G[Remove Specimen]
    G --> H[Measure Indentation Diameter d1 using Microscope]
    H --> I[Measure Perpendicular Diameter d2]
    I --> J[Compute Mean d = d1 + d2 / 2]
    J --> K[Apply Formula HB = 2F over piD times D minus sqrt D squared minus d squared]
    K --> L[Record HBW or HBS Value]
    L --> M[Report Hardness with Test Conditions]
    M --> N[End]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Vickers Test Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Polish Specimen to Mirror Finish] --> B[Mount on Vickers Anvil]
    B --> C[Select Load via Selector Dial]
    C --> D[Activate Loading Lever]
    D --> E[Indenter Pyramid Descends Slowly]
    E --> F[Dwell 10-15 s]
    F --> G[Unload Automatically]
    G --> H[Transfer Specimen to Measuring Microscope]
    H --> I[Align Diagonal d1 Crosshair]
    I --> J[Read d1 from Micrometer Scale]
    J --> K[Rotate 90 deg for d2]
    K --> L[Compute Mean d]
    L --> M[Apply Formula HV = 1.8544F over d squared]
    M --> N[Report HV with Load]
    N --> O[End]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Rockwell Test Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Place Specimen on Anvil] --> B[Anvil Rises to Contact Indenter]
    B --> C[Apply Minor Load F0 = 10 kgf]
    C --> D[Set Dial Gauge to Zero Reference]
    D --> E[Apply Major Load F1 via Lever]
    E --> F[Dwell 2-6 s]
    F --> G[Remove Major Load Automatically]
    G --> H[Dial Reads Permanent Depth e]
    H --> I[Compute HR using N minus e over k]
    I --> J[Report HR with Scale Letter and Indenter]
    J --> K[End]
```

flowchart LR

flowchart TD

    B -- Yes --> C[Use HRA Scale 60 kgf diamond]
    B -- No --> D{Is it Hardened Tool Steel 30 to 65 HRC}

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define hardness. List the three main indentation hardness tests with their respective indenters and loads.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Hardness** is defined as the resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation caused by mechanical indentation, scratching, or abrasion. The three main indentation hardness tests are:
>
> 1. **Brinell Hardness Test** — uses a hardened steel or tungsten carbide ball indenter of standard diameter (typically 10 mm) with loads ranging from 1 to 3000 kgf.
> 2. **Vickers Hardness Test** — uses a square-based diamond pyramid indenter with a 136° face angle, with loads from 1 to 120 kgf.
> 3. **Rockwell Hardness Test** — uses either a diamond cone (120° included angle) or a steel ball indenter, with major loads of 60, 100, or 150 kgf applied over a 10 kgf minor load.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State the Vickers hardness formula. What is the significance of the constant 1.8544 in this formula?**

**Model Answer:**

The Vickers hardness number is given by:

$$HV = \frac{1.8544 \times F}{d^2}$$

where $F$ is the applied load in kgf and $d$ is the mean diagonal of the indentation in mm.

> The constant **1.8544** originates from the geometry of the diamond pyramid indenter. Specifically, it equals $2 \sin(\theta/2)$ where $\theta = 136°$ is the face-to-face angle. This value makes HV numerically comparable to HB for soft-to-medium hard materials, ensuring continuity of the historical hardness scale. It also arises from using the slanted (surface) area of the pyramid impression as the reference area, similar to Brinell's surface-area convention.

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the Brinell hardness test in detail. Explain the Brinell hardness formula, the significance of the $F/D^2$ ratio, and the standard conditions of the test.**

**Model Answer:**

**Description of the Brinell Test:**

The Brinell hardness test, standardized under **ASTM E10**, was developed by Johan August Brinell in 1900. It is the oldest standardized indentation hardness test and is widely used for materials with coarse or heterogeneous microstructures such as cast irons, forgings, and rough-surfaced components.

**Procedure:**
1. The specimen surface is prepared by grinding or machining to obtain a flat, reasonably smooth surface (no need for mirror finish).
2. A hardened steel ball (HBS) or tungsten carbide ball (HBW) of diameter $D$ (commonly **10 mm**, but 5 mm, 2.5 mm, or 1 mm may be used) is pressed into the specimen under a static load $F$.
3. The load is held for a **dwell time** of typically **10–15 seconds for steel** and **30 seconds for non-ferrous metals**.
4. After load removal, the diameter $d$ of the circular indentation is measured optically using a **low-power traveling microscope** with a graduated reticle. Two perpendicular diameter measurements $d_1$ and $d_2$ are taken and averaged.
5. The Brinell hardness number is calculated.

**Brinell Hardness Formula:**

$$HB = \frac{2F}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}$$

This formula is derived from the ratio of the applied load to the **curved surface area** of the indentation (spherical cap), with the geometry yielding a depth $h = (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})/2$.

**Significance of the $F/D^2$ Ratio:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ratio $F/D^2$ is the **single most critical parameter** in Brinell testing. For results to be comparable across different specimen sizes, materials, and laboratories, this ratio must be held **constant**.

The standard values are:
- **$F/D^2 = 30$** for steels, cast irons, and hard alloys.
- **$F/D^2 = 10$** for copper, brass, and bronze.
- **$F/D^2 = 5$** for aluminium, lead, tin, and other soft non-ferrous metals.

This ensures **geometrically similar indentations** (i.e., the ratio $d/D$ is approximately constant for a given material), satisfying the requirements of the **Bucknell's law of similarity** for indentation mechanics. If $F/D^2$ is too high, the ball may flatten or the specimen may fracture; if too low, the impression will be too small to measure accurately.

**Standard Conditions:**

| **Parameter** | **Standard Value** |
|---------------|---------------------|
| Ball diameter $D$ | 10 mm (most common) |
| Indenter material | Hardened steel (HBS) ≥ 850 HV; Tungsten carbide (HBW) |
| Load $F$ | 3000 kgf (steels), 1000 kgf (Cu alloys), 500 kgf (Al) |
| Dwell time | 10–15 s (steel), 30 s (non-ferrous) |
| Indentation diameter $d$ | Should be 0.25$D$ < $d$ < 0.5$D$ for valid reading |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A Brinell hardness test is conducted on a steel specimen using a 10 mm ball and 3000 kgf load. The measured indentation diameter is 4.2 mm. Calculate the Brinell hardness number. Also calculate the depth of indentation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 10 \text{ mm}$, $F = 3000 \text{ kgf}$, $d = 4.2 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1: Calculate the depth of indentation $h$.**

The depth of indentation is given by:

$$h = \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}$$

Compute $D^2 - d^2$:

$$D^2 - d^2 = 100 - 17.64 = 82.36 \text{ mm}^2$$

Compute the square root:

$$\sqrt{82.36} = 9.0752 \text{ mm}$$

Compute the bracket term:

$$D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} = 10 - 9.0752 = 0.9248 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:

$$h = \frac{0.9248}{2} = 0.4624 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Brinell Hardness Number.**

$$HB = \frac{2F}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\pi \times 10 \times 0.9248 = 29.054 \text{ mm}^2$$

Compute the numerator:

$$2 \times 3000 = 6000 \text{ kgf}$$

Therefore:

$$HB = \frac{6000}{29.054} = 206.5 \text{ kgf/mm}^2$$

**Result:** $\boxed{HB = 206.5 \text{ kgf/mm}^2}$ and $\boxed{h = 0.4624 \text{ mm}}$

> [!NOTE]
> This is consistent with a medium-carbon steel in the normalized condition (typical HB range 180–230).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the Vickers hardness test in detail. Derive the Vickers hardness formula and explain why the face angle of the indenter is 136°.**

**Model Answer:**

**Description of the Vickers Test:**

The Vickers hardness test, standardized under **ASTM E92**, was developed in 1924 at Vickers Ltd. (UK) as an improvement over the Brinell test. It uses a **square-based diamond pyramid** indenter and can be used over an enormous hardness range — from very soft metals (~5 HV) to hardened tool steel and even cemented carbides (~2000 HV).

**Procedure:**
1. The specimen is polished to a **mirror finish** to allow accurate optical measurement of the small indentation diagonals.
2. The diamond pyramid indenter (136° face angle) is pressed into the surface under a chosen load $F$ (typically 1, 5, 10, 30, 50, or 100 kgf).
3. After a dwell time of **10–15 seconds**, the load is removed.
4. The indentation is a **square impression** with two perpendicular diagonals $d_1$ and $d_2$.
5. The diagonals are measured using a **high-power optical microscope**, and the mean diagonal $d = (d_1 + d_2)/2$ is computed.
6. The Vickers hardness is calculated from the formula.

**Derivation of the Vickers Formula:**

The **surface area** of the square pyramid impression is:

$$A_s = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$$

For $\theta = 136°$:

$$\sin(68°) = 0.9272$$

$$A_s = \frac{d^2}{2 \times 0.9272} = \frac{d^2}{1.8544}$$

By definition, Vickers hardness equals load divided by surface area:

$$HV = \frac{F}{A_s} = \frac{1.8544 \times F}{d^2}$$

**Why 136°?**

The choice of **136°** face angle was deliberate and historical. It gives the Vickers hardness number a value that **matches the Brinell hardness number up to approximately 300 HB**. This continuity was important in 1924 when Vickers was introduced as a successor to Brinell — users could directly compare new Vickers readings to old Brinell records without needing conversion tables for the common engineering range.

Mathematically, the choice $\theta = 136°$ makes $\sin(\theta/2) = \sin(68°) \approx 0.9272$, giving the convenient constant $1.8544 = 2 \sin(68°)$.

**Advantages of the 136° Angle:**
- The resulting impressions are **clearly visible** (not too small, not too large).
