
> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Lab Tip:**
> In a real experiment, the dial gauge reading (lateral deflection) remains nearly zero until the critical load is reached, after which it increases rapidly even if the load is held constant. This is the signature of elastic instability.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define the term "Slenderness Ratio" of a column and state its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The slenderness ratio (λ) of a column is defined as the ratio of its effective length to its radius of gyration:
$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k} = \frac{L_e}{\sqrt{I/A}}$$
It is a **dimensionless** quantity. Its significance lies in determining the failure mode of the column: a high λ (> λc) indicates that the column is slender and will fail by **Euler buckling**, while a low λ (< λc) indicates a short column that will fail by **material crushing**. It helps engineers select the appropriate design formula (Euler's or Rankine's).

**Q2. State Euler's formula for critical buckling load for a column with both ends fixed. Why is the effective length shorter than the actual length in this case?**

**Model Answer:**
For a column with both ends fixed, Euler's formula is:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{(L/2)^2} = \frac{4\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$
The effective length is **L/2 (n = 0.5)** because fixed ends prevent both deflection and rotation, effectively halving the buckling span. The column buckles in a shape with two inflection points, making the unsupported buckling length equal to half the actual length. Hence, fixed-end columns have a **higher critical load** (4×) than pinned-end columns of the same length.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]*

**Q3. (a)** A mild steel strut of rectangular cross-section 40 mm × 20 mm and length 1.6 m is pin-ended at both ends. Find:
(i) The Euler critical buckling load
(ii) The critical stress at buckling
(iii) The slenderness ratio

Take E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm². Comment on the validity of Euler's theory in this case.

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Moment of Inertia (minimum value)**

For buckling, the column deflects about the weaker axis. The minimum I corresponds to bending about the axis parallel to the longer dimension (b = 40 mm):

$$I_{min} = \frac{40 \times 20^3}{12} = \frac{40 \times 8000}{12} = 26{,}667 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2: Euler Critical Load (Pinned-Pinned, n = 1)**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2} = \frac{(3.1416)^2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 26667}{(1600)^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 26667}{2.56 \times 10^6}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{5.2633 \times 10^{10}}{2.56 \times 10^6} = 20{,}560 \text{ N} \approx 20.56 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Critical Stress**

$$A = 40 \times 20 = 800 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{20560}{800} = 25.7 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 25.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Slenderness Ratio**

$$k = \sqrt{I/A} = \sqrt{26667/800} = \sqrt{33.33} = 5.77 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{L}{k} = \frac{1600}{5.77} = 277$$

**Step 5: Validity Comment**

Since λ = 277 is much greater than λc (≈ 79 for mild steel), **Euler's theory is fully valid**. The column will fail by elastic buckling at a stress (25.7 MPa) far below the yield stress of mild steel.

---

**(b)** In a strut test experiment, a flat mild steel specimen of length 600 mm, width 25 mm, and thickness 4 mm is tested with both ends pin-connected. The experimental buckling load recorded is 1.2 kN. Compare this with:
(i) The theoretical Euler load
(ii) Rankine's load (Take a = 1/7500, σc = 320 MPa)

Calculate the percentage deviation in each case and comment on the results.

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Geometry Properties**

$$A = 25 \times 4 = 100 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$I_{min} = \frac{25 \times 4^3}{12} = \frac{25 \times 64}{12} = 133.33 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$k = \sqrt{I/A} = \sqrt{133.33/100} = \sqrt{1.333} = 1.155 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{600}{1.155} = 519.6$$

**Step 2: Euler Theoretical Load**

$$P_E = \frac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 133.33}{600^2} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 133.33}{360000}$$

$$P_E = \frac{2.6317 \times 10^9}{360000} = 7310 \text{ N} \approx 7.31 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Rankine Load**

$$P_R = \frac{320 \times 100}{1 + \frac{1}{7500}(519.6)^2} = \frac{32000}{1 + \frac{269984}{7500}}$$

$$P_R = \frac{32000}{1 + 35.998} = \frac{32000}{36.998} = 865 \text{ N} \approx 0.865 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Percentage Errors**

Euler: $\dfrac{7.31 - 1.2}{7.31} \times 100 = 83.6\%$ (Euler overestimates)

Rankine: $\dfrac{0.865 - 1.2}{1.2} \times 100 = -27.9\%$ (Rankine underestimates)

**Step 5: Comment**

The large deviation indicates that the experimental specimen likely had **initial imperfections** (slight curvature, eccentric loading, or material inhomogeneity), causing premature buckling. In practice, **experimental buckling loads are always less than theoretical Euler loads** due to real-world imperfections. The Rankine formula gives a much closer estimate when material crushing effects are accounted for.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]*

**Q4. (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **experimental setup and procedure** of a Strut Test to determine the critical buckling load of a slender column. List at least four observations and precautions.

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Experimental Setup:**
The Strut Test apparatus typically consists of:
1. A **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** with hydraulic loading system.
2. **Pin-ended (hinged) grippers** at both ends of the specimen, allowing rotation but no translation.
3. A **load cell** to measure the applied compressive load.
4. A **dial gauge** mounted at the mid-span of the specimen to measure lateral deflection.
5. A set of **flat mild steel specimens** of varying lengths (commonly 300 mm, 500 mm, 700 mm, 900 mm) but same cross-section.

**Procedure:**
1. Measure the dimensions (length L, width b, thickness h) of the specimen accurately using a vernier caliper.
2. Calculate the moment of inertia (I) and slenderness ratio (λ).
3. Fix the specimen firmly between the two pin-ended grips, ensuring **perfect axial alignment**.
4. Zero the load indicator and the dial gauge.
5. Apply a small pre-load (≈ 5% of expected failure load) to seat the specimen properly.
6. Increase the load gradually in small increments (e.g., 50 N steps).
7. At each increment, record the load reading and the corresponding lateral deflection.
8. Continue until **sudden buckling** is observed; record the corresponding critical load.
9. Unload the specimen carefully and repeat with different lengths.

**Observations and Precautions:**
- The dial gauge reading remains nearly zero until the **critical load** is approached.
- After buckling, even a small increase in load causes **large lateral deflections**.
- The specimen must be **perfectly straight** initially; any curvature reduces the buckling load.
- Loading must be **truly concentric** (axial); eccentric loading causes premature failure.
- Use a **long slender specimen** so that λ >> λc and Euler buckling dominates.

---

**(b)** A **hollow circular steel column** of external diameter 60 mm, internal diameter 50 mm, and length 2 m is fixed at one end and free at the other. Find:
(i) The Euler critical load
(ii) The critical stress
(iii) Compare with the load if both ends were pin-connected.

Take E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm².

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Section Properties**

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}(60^4 - 50^4)$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(12.96 \times 10^6 - 6.25 \times 10^6) = \frac{\pi}{64}(6.71 \times 10^6)$$

$$I = 3.293 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(3600 - 2500) = \frac{\pi}{4}(1100) = 863.94 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Case 1 — Fixed-Free (n = 2)**

$$P_{cr,1} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{(2L)^2} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 3.293 \times 10^5}{(2 \times 2000)^2}$$

$$P_{cr,1} = \frac{6.498 \times 10^{11}}{1.6 \times 10^7} = 40{,}610 \text{ N} \approx 40.61 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Critical Stress (Fixed-Free)**

$$\sigma_{cr,1} = \frac{P_{cr,1}}{A} = \frac{40610}{863.94} = 47.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Case 2 — Both Ends Pinned (n = 1)**

$$P_{cr,2} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 3.293 \times 10^5}{(2000)^2}$$

$$P_{cr,2} = \frac{6.498 \times 10^{11}}{4 \times 10^6} = 1{,}62{,}440 \text{ N} \approx 162.44 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Comparison**

$$\frac{P_{cr,2}}{P_{cr,1}} = \frac{162.44}{40.61} = 4$$

The pin-ended column can carry **4 times more load** than the fixed-free column of the same length, because its effective length is halved. This shows that **end conditions drastically affect buckling capacity** — a key principle in column design.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Strut Test Questions:**
> 
> **1. Choosing the wrong moment of inertia (–3 to –4 marks):**
> Students often use I = bh³/12 without identifying that **Imin** governs buckling. For a rectangular bar 25 × 6, bending is easier about the 25-axis (smaller thickness 6 mm in the bending direction). Always pick the axis with the **smaller I**.
> 
> **2. Confusing Slenderness Ratio λ with Length Ratio (–2 marks):**
> λ = L_e / k, **not** L/b or L/h. Many students write λ = L/b and lose marks.
> 
> **3. Forgetting the end-condition factor n (–2 marks):**
> If the problem says "one end fixed, other free," use Le = 2L, not L. The factor n is crucial.
> 
> **4. Unit Inconsistency (–1 mark):**
> E in N/mm², I in mm⁴, L in mm → P in N. Mixing units (e.g., E in Pa and L in m while I in mm⁴) leads to wrong answers.
> 
> **5. Euler validity check missing (–1 mark):**
> Always compute λ and compare with λc ≈ 79 for mild steel. If λ < λc, state that Euler's theory is **not applicable** and Rankine's formula must be used.
> 
> **6. Plotting the wrong graph in viva (–2 marks):**
> KTU expects **Load (P) on y-axis vs Lateral Deflection (δ) on x-axis**, with the curve showing a sharp rise at P = Pcr.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Euler's Critical Load (Pinned-Pinned):** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$
- **Generalized form:** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$, with $L_e = nL$
- **End condition factors:** Pinned-Pinned = 1, Fixed-Fixed = 0.5, Fixed-Pinned ≈ 0.7, Fixed-Free = 2
- **Slenderness Ratio:** $\lambda = L_e / k$, where $k = \sqrt{I/A}$
- **Critical Slenderness Ratio for Mild Steel:** $\lambda_c \approx 79$
- **Validity Rule:** Use Euler only when **λ > λc**; otherwise use Rankine's formula.
- **Rankine's Formula:** $P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c A}{1 + a(L_e/k)^2}$
- **Buckling Stress:** $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^2 E / \lambda^2$
- **Experimental Procedure:** Measure dimensions → calculate I, A, λ → fix in UTM with pin ends → apply gradual load → record critical buckling load.
- **Typical Specimens:** Mild steel flat bars, lengths 300–1000 mm, cross-sections 25×6 mm or similar.
- **Always plot:** Load (P) vs Lateral Deflection (δ) — curve shows sharp peak at Pcr.
- **Always state:** Comparison between theoretical and experimental values, percentage error, and reasons for deviation (imperfections, eccentric loading, friction at supports).
- **Real-world takeaway:** Buckling is a **geometry-dominated failure** — increasing length dramatically reduces capacity; increasing thickness dramatically increases capacity.
- **KTU Viva Favorite Questions:**
  1. *"Why does the dial gauge reading stay near zero until the critical load?"* → Because the column is stable and follows Hooke's Law with negligible lateral movement.
  2. *"Why is Euler's formula not valid for short columns?"* → Because the material yields before elastic instability can occur, violating Euler's elastic assumption.
  3. *"What is the physical meaning of radius of gyration?"* → It represents how far the cross-sectional area is concentrated from the neutral axis; a higher k means more resistance to buckling.
  4. *"How does a hollow section compare with a solid section of the same weight?"* → Hollow sections have higher I for the same A, hence greater buckling resistance — this is why tubes are preferred in structural applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:**
> In your Strut Test viva and record, always include:
> (1) Aim and Objective,
> (2) Apparatus list with neat sketch,
> (3) Theory with Euler's formula,
> (4) Tabulated observations (Length, Cross-section, λ, P_exp, P_Euler, P_Rankine),
> (5) Sample calculations showing all steps,
> (6) Graph of P vs δ,
> (7) Result table with percentage error,
> (8) Conclusion stating validity of Euler's theory and sources of error.
> Missing any of these will cost you 2–3 marks easily.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Moment of Inertia of Rotating Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL308 - CONTINUUM MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Moment of Inertia of Rotating Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moment of Inertia (I)** of a rotating body, about a given axis, is defined as the **summation of the products of the mass of every elementary particle of the body and the square of its perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation**.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, r_i^2 \quad \longrightarrow \quad I = \int r^2 \, dm$$

The **SI unit** is **kg·m²** and its **dimensional formula** is **[M L² T⁰]**.

It is the **rotational analogue of mass** in translational motion. Just as mass resists a change in linear velocity (Newton's First Law), the moment of inertia resists a change in rotational velocity (angular velocity).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ice Skater Spin"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine an ice skater spinning on ice. When she stretches her arms wide, she spins slowly. When she pulls her arms close to her body, she spins very fast — even though no external torque acted on her. The "resistance to spinning" (moment of inertia) changes with the **distribution of mass about the spin axis**. Mass far from the axis = high I = slow spin. Mass close to the axis = low I = fast spin.

In this experiment, we exploit the **rotational dynamics** of a rigid body driven by a falling weight to compute I experimentally.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | **9.81 m/s²** | m/s² |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | P₀ | 1.013 × 10⁵ | Pa |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Experimental Principle — Two Complementary Approaches

**Approach A — Energy Conservation Principle (Most common in KTU labs):**
A mass $m$ attached to a string wound around an axle of radius $r$ is allowed to fall. As it falls through height $h$, the body (disc/flywheel) rotates. Energy is conserved because the string is assumed inextensible and the axle frictionless at the bearing.

**Approach B — Newton's Second Law (Dynamic/Force Method):**
Using the equations of motion for the falling mass and the rotating body simultaneously, we set up coupled equations and eliminate the unknown string tension $T'$.

### 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. A **string** is wound around the axle of radius $r$ fixed to the rotating body.
2. A **known mass $m$** is tied to the free end of the string.
3. The mass is **released from rest** and allowed to fall through a measured height $h$.
4. The **time $t$** for the fall is recorded using a stopwatch.
5. The body rotates with angular velocity $\omega$ as the mass reaches the bottom.
6. From kinematics: $a = \dfrac{2h}{t^2}$ and $v = \dfrac{2h}{t}$.
7. The **no-slip condition** relates linear and angular velocity: $v = r\omega$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they form the backbone of any numerical from this experiment.**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $I = \sum m_i r_i^2$ | Definition of MI | kg·m² |
| 2 | $v = r\omega$ | No-slip (rolling) condition | m/s, rad/s |
| 3 | $a = r\alpha$ | Linear-angular acceleration link | m/s², rad/s² |
| 4 | $a = \dfrac{2h}{t^2}$ | Kinematic relation for free-fall start | m/s² |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{2h}{t}$ | Final velocity after fall | m/s |
| 6 | $mgh = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 + \dfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ | Energy conservation (KEY EQUATION) | J |
| 7 | $I = mr^2 \left[ \dfrac{gt^2}{2h} - 1 \right]$ | Working formula (energy method) | kg·m² |
| 8 | $T' \cdot r = I\alpha$ | Rotational form of Newton's 2nd Law | N·m |
| 9 | $mg - T' = ma$ | Linear motion of falling mass | N |
| 10 | $I = \dfrac{mr^2(g-a)}{a}$ | Working formula (force method) | kg·m² |

> ⚠️ **Critical Reminder for Tables:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for any absolute value bars — never raw `|` — to keep the markdown table syntax intact.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flywheel design** in IC engines, press machinery, and energy storage systems.
- **Turbine rotor balancing** in power plants.
- **Aerospace:** satellite attitude control and gyroscope calibration.
- **Automotive:** brake disc, wheel rim, and crankshaft design.
- **Sports engineering:** moment of inertia of cricket bats, tennis rackets, and racing bicycles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Working Formula via Energy Conservation

> **Starting Point:** A mass $m$ falls through height $h$ and rotates a body of moment of inertia $I$ about an axle of radius $r$.

**Step 1 — Write Energy Conservation Equation.**
The loss in **gravitational potential energy** of the falling mass must equal the gain in **kinetic energy** of the mass (linear) plus the kinetic energy of the rotating body (rotational).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loss in PE} &= \text{Linear KE of mass} + \text{Rotational KE of body} \\
mgh &= \frac{1}{2} m v^2 + \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \quad \text{...(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the No-Slip Condition.**
Because the string is inextensible and wound on the axle, the linear velocity of the falling mass equals the tangential velocity at the axle rim.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= r \omega \\
\Rightarrow \omega &= \frac{v}{r} \quad \text{...(ii)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (ii) into (i) to eliminate $\omega$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
mgh &= \frac{1}{2} m v^2 + \frac{1}{2} I \left(\frac{v}{r}\right)^2 \\
mgh &= \frac{1}{2} m v^2 + \frac{1}{2} \frac{I v^2}{r^2} \quad \text{...(iii)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express $v$ in terms of measured quantities.**
Since the mass starts from rest ($u = 0$) and travels height $h$ in time $t$ under uniform acceleration $a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= ut + \frac{1}{2} a t^2 = \frac{1}{2} a t^2 \quad (\text{since } u = 0) \\
\Rightarrow a &= \frac{2h}{t^2} \quad \text{...(iv)} \\[4pt]
v &= u + at = at = \frac{2h}{t} \quad \text{...(v)} \\
\Rightarrow v^2 &= \frac{4h^2}{t^2} \quad \text{...(vi)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute (vi) into (iii) and solve for $I$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
mgh &= \frac{1}{2} m \cdot \frac{4h^2}{t^2} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{I}{r^2} \cdot \frac{4h^2}{t^2} \\
mgh &= \frac{2 m h^2}{t^2} + \frac{2 I h^2}{r^2 t^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Divide both sides by $h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
mg &= \frac{2 m h}{t^2} + \frac{2 I h}{r^2 t^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply both sides by $\dfrac{r^2 t^2}{2h}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{mg \cdot r^2 t^2}{2h} &= m r^2 + I \\
\boxed{\, I = m r^2 \left[ \frac{g t^2}{2h} - 1 \right] \,}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **master working formula** for the experiment.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cross-Check via Newton's Second Law (Force Method)

**Step 1 — Equation of motion for the falling mass (translation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
mg - T' &= ma \quad \text{...(a)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equation of motion for the rotating body (rotation about axle):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T' \cdot r &= I \alpha = I \cdot \frac{a}{r} \quad \text{...(b)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — From (b), express $T'$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T' &= \frac{I a}{r^2} \quad \text{...(c)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (c) into (a):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
mg - \frac{I a}{r^2} &= m a \\
mg &= m a + \frac{I a}{r^2} = a \left( m + \frac{I}{r^2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $I$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{2h}{t^2} \\
I &= r^2 \left( \frac{mg}{a} - m \right) = r^2 m \left( \frac{g}{a} - 1 \right) = r^2 m \left( \frac{gt^2}{2h} - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Both methods yield the **identical working formula**, confirming internal consistency of the analysis.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A flywheel of axle diameter 4 cm has a 0.5 kg mass hanging from a string wound around it. The mass falls 1.0 m in 2.5 s starting from rest. Calculate the moment of inertia of the flywheel. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Given:** $m = 0.5$ kg, $d = 4$ cm $\Rightarrow r = 0.02$ m, $h = 1.0$ m, $t = 2.5$ s, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Compute the term $\dfrac{gt^2}{2h}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{g t^2}{2h} &= \frac{9.81 \times (2.5)^2}{2 \times 1.0} = \frac{9.81 \times 6.25}{2.0} = \frac{61.3125}{2.0} = 30.656
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{g t^2}{2h} - 1 &= 30.656 - 1 = 29.656
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $m r^2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
mr^2 &= 0.5 \times (0.02)^2 = 0.5 \times 0.0004 = 0.0002 \text{ kg·m}^2 \\
I &= 0.0002 \times 29.656 = 5.931 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg·m}^2 \\
\boxed{\, I \approx 5.93 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg·m}^2 \,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Error Check** (if theoretical value given, say $I_{th} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}$ kg·m²):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Error} &= \left| \frac{I_{exp} - I_{th}}{I_{th}} \right| \times 100 = \left| \frac{5.93 - 6.0}{6.0} \right| \times 100 \approx 1.17\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> A percentage error **below 5%** is typically considered excellent in KTU lab evaluations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Experimental Rig

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **functional interaction** of every component in the experimental setup, including measurement feedback loops and energy flow paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PowerSource[Mass m at Rest] --> StringCouple[Wound String on Axle]
    StringCouple --> AxleDrive[Axle of Radius r]
    AxleDrive --> FlywheelDisc[Flywheel / Rotating Body]
    FlywheelDisc --> BearingSupport[Low-Friction Bearing]
    BearingSupport --> StandBase[Fixed Vertical Stand]
    StringCouple --> FreeFallTrack[Vertical Free-Fall Track h]
    FreeFallTrack --> StopwatchTrigger[Stopwatch Start and Stop]
    StopwatchTrigger --> TimeReadout[Time t measured]
    AxleDrive --> VelocityLink[v = r omega, a = r alpha]
    VelocityLink --> EnergyBalance[Energy Conservation Equation]
    EnergyBalance --> ResultCompute[Working Formula I = m r² gt² 2h minus 1]
    TimeReadout --> ResultCompute
    ResultCompute --> FinalOutput[Moment of Inertia I in kg m²]
    ResultCompute --> PercentError[Percentage Error vs Theory]
    PercentError --> FinalOutput
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the Physical Setup (Text-Based Cross-Section)

```
                 ┌───────────────────────┐
                 │   FIXED VERTICAL      │
                 │   SUPPORT STAND       │
                 │        ║              │
                 │        ║              │
                 │   ┌────╨────┐         │
                 │   │ FLYWHEEL│         │
                 │   │  (Disc) │         │
                 │   │    ●←──────── Axle (radius r) ────────●───┐
                 │   └────┥────┘         │                    │   │
                 │        ┃              │                    │   │
                 │   BEARING             │                    │ STRING (wound)
                 │        ┃              │                    │   │
                 │        ┃              │                    │   │
                 │   ┌────╨────┐         │                    │   │
                 │   │  AXLE   │         │                    │   │
                 │   └────┥────┘         │                    │   │
                 │        ┃              │                    │   │
                 │   ┌────╨────┐         │                    │   │
                 │   │  MASS m │  ←──── falls through height h ───┘
                 │   └─────────┘         │
                 │     ↕  h (measured)   │
                 └───────────────────────┘
```

### 4.3 Data Acquisition Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Student
    participant A as Apparatus
    participant T as Timer
    participant D as Data Sheet
    S->>A: Wind string around axle
    S->>A: Attach mass m to free end
    S->>A: Hold mass at top mark
    S->>T: Start stopwatch
    S->>A: Release mass
    A-->>T: Mass falls height h
    T->>S: Record time t
    S->>D: Note m, r, h, t values
    D->>D: Compute acceleration a = 2h/t²
    D->>D: Compute v = 2h/t
    D->>D: Apply I = mr²(gt²/2h − 1)
    D-->>S: Final I and percentage error
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the term **Moment of Inertia** of a rigid body. State its SI unit and the factors on which it depends.

> **Model Answer:**
> The moment of inertia of a rigid body about a given axis is the sum of the products of the mass of every elementary particle and the square of its perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation.
> $I = \sum m_i r_i^2$
> **SI unit:** kg·m²
> It depends upon: (1) mass of the body, (2) distribution of mass about the axis, (3) position and orientation of the axis of rotation. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State the **principle of conservation of energy** as applied to a falling mass driving a rotating body. Write the resulting energy balance equation.

> **Model Answer:**
> The principle states that in the absence of non-conservative forces (like friction), the total mechanical energy of an isolated system remains constant. For the experiment:
> Loss in PE of mass = Gain in KE (linear) of mass + Gain in KE (rotational) of body
> $mgh = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 + \dfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$
> Using $v = r\omega$: $mgh = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(m + \dfrac{I}{r^2}\right)v^2$ **(3 marks)**

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]

**(a)** Derive the working formula for the moment of inertia of a rotating body using the **energy conservation method**, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment, a mass of **0.6 kg** falls through **0.8 m** in **2.0 s** from rest, turning a body via a string wound on an axle of diameter **5 cm**. Calculate the moment of inertia of the body. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for (a):**
>
> **Assumptions:**
> 1. String is inextensible and massless.
> 2. Axle is frictionless at the bearing.
> 3. The body is rigid; no energy loss as heat or sound.
> 4. The mass starts from rest and falls under uniform acceleration.
>
> **Derivation:**
> Let mass $m$ fall through height $h$ in time $t$, rotating a body of moment of inertia $I$ about an axle of radius $r$.
> By conservation of energy:
> $mgh = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 + \dfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$  ...(1)
> No-slip condition: $v = r\omega$ ...(2)
> From kinematics: $v = \dfrac{2h}{t}$, so $v^2 = \dfrac{4h^2}{t^2}$ ...(3)
> Substituting (2) and (3) into (1):
> $mgh = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot \dfrac{4h^2}{t^2} + \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{4h^2}{r^2 t^2}$
> $mg = \dfrac{2mh}{t^2} + \dfrac{2Ih}{r^2 t^2}$
> Solving for $I$: $\boxed{I = mr^2\left(\dfrac{gt^2}{2h} - 1\right)}$ **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for (b):**
>
> **Given:** $m = 0.6$ kg, $h = 0.8$ m, $t = 2.0$ s, $r = \dfrac{0.05}{2} = 0.025$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
>
> **Step 1 — Compute $\dfrac{gt^2}{2h}$:**
> $\dfrac{9.81 \times (2.0)^2}{2 \times 0.8} = \dfrac{9.81 \times 4}{1.6} = \dfrac{39.24}{1.6} = 24.525$
>
> **Step 2 — Subtract 1:** $24.525 - 1 = 23.525$
>
> **Step 3 — Compute $mr^2$:** $0.6 \times (0.025)^2 = 0.6 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4}$ kg·m²
>
> **Step 4 — Final I:** $I = 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \times 23.525 = 8.82 \times 10^{-3}$ kg·m²
> $\boxed{I \approx 8.82 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg·m}^2}$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the experimental setup used to determine the moment of inertia of a rotating body. List the observations and measurements required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Newton's second law** for translation and rotation, derive the working formula for $I$ and explain how it matches the energy method result. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for (a):**
>
> **Apparatus:** Vertical stand with a low-friction bearing, a flywheel/disc rigidly fixed to a horizontal axle of known radius $r$, an inextensible string wound on the axle, slotted masses (or a hanger with weights), a stopwatch, and a metre scale.
>
> **Procedure / Observations:**
> 1. Measure axle diameter using vernier calipers to find $r$.
> 2. Note the mass $m$ attached at the end of the string.
> 3. Mark a fixed vertical height $h$ (e.g., 1.0 m) along the stand.
> 4. Wind the string fully on the axle; release the mass from rest.
> 5. Record time $t$ for the mass to fall through $h$ using the stopwatch.
> 6. Repeat 3–4 times and take the mean value of $t$.
> 7. Vary $m$ (or $h$) to obtain multiple data sets.
>
> **Sketch:** As shown in SECTION 4.2 above. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for (b):**
>
> **Equations of motion:**
> For the falling mass: $mg - T' = ma$ ...(1)
> For the rotating body: $T' \cdot r = I\alpha$, with $\alpha = a/r$, giving $T' = Ia/r^2$ ...(2)
> Substitute (2) into (1):
> $mg - \dfrac{Ia}{r^2} = ma \Rightarrow mg = a\left(m + \dfrac{I}{r^2}\right)$
> Using $a = \dfrac{2h}{t^2}$:
> $mg = \dfrac{2h}{t^2}\left(m + \dfrac{I}{r^2}\right)$
> $mg t^2 = 2h \left(m + \dfrac{I}{r^2}\right)$
> $\dfrac{mg t^2}{2h} = m + \dfrac{I}{r^2}$
> $I = r^2\left(\dfrac{mg t^2}{2h} - m\right) = mr^2\left(\dfrac{gt^2}{2h} - 1\right)$
>
> **Match with energy method:** The resulting expression is **identical** to that obtained via energy conservation, confirming that both approaches are mathematically equivalent descriptions of the same physical phenomenon. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this experiment:**
> 1. **Forgetting to halve the diameter for radius** ($r = d/2$ is a frequent error).
> 2. **Using the full distance as $h$** when the mass was released from a position below the axle — measure the *actual* vertical fall.
> 3. **Neglecting string thickness** when wound around the axle — this changes the effective radius.
> 4. **Not taking the mean of multiple readings** for $t$ — KTU examiners look for repeatability.
> 5. **Failing to convert units** (cm to m) before substituting into the formula.
> 6. **Using $g = 10$ m/s² silently** — always declare the value of $g$ used; examiners appreciate precision.
> 7. **Skipping the percentage error calculation** in the viva — this is a guaranteed 2-mark differentiator.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Moment of Inertia** is the rotational equivalent of mass, given by $I = \sum m_i r_i^2$.
- ✅ **SI Unit:** kg·m². **Dimensional formula:** [M L² T⁰].
- ✅ The experiment exploits the **no-slip condition** $v = r\omega$ and **kinematic relation** $v = \dfrac{2h}{t}$ (starting from rest).
- ✅ **Master formula:** $I = mr^2 \left( \dfrac{gt^2}{2h} - 1 \right)$.
- ✅ **Two equivalent derivations:** Energy method and Newton's second law method both yield the **same working formula**.
- ✅ **Percentage error** below **5%** is the KTU benchmark for a well-performed experiment.
- ✅ **Assumptions:** inextensible string, frictionless bearing, rigid body, mass starts from rest.
- ✅ **Common axes of rotation:** central axis of a disc, axis of a solid sphere, axis through the centre perpendicular to the length of a rod.
- ✅ **Theoretical formulas for quick comparison:**
    - Solid disc (axis through centre, perpendicular to plane): $I = \dfrac{1}{2}MR^2$
    - Solid sphere (diameter axis): $I = \dfrac{2}{5}MR^2$
    - Thin rod (axis through one end, perpendicular to length): $I = \dfrac{1}{3}ML^2$
- ✅ **Repeat each timing at least 3 times** and tabulate mean values — this is a KTU lab-record expectation.
- ✅ **Vernier calipers** are used to measure axle diameter; **metre scale** for height; **stopwatch** for time.
- ✅ **Practical tip:** If $t$ is too small, increase $h$ or use a smaller $m$ to keep $t$ in the **2–5 second** range for accurate stopwatch readings.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue Life (Endurance) Test
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL308 - CONTINUUM MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Fatigue Life (Endurance) Test

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fatigue:** It is the process of progressive, localized permanent structural change occurring in a material subjected to fluctuating stresses and strains at points or regions where strain concentrations exist. Failure occurs after a sufficient number of fluctuations, even though the **maximum stress** experienced is well below the material's static yield strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Endurance Limit (Se):** The maximum completely reversed bending stress that a material can withstand for an infinite number (typically ≥ 10⁶ to 10⁷) of stress cycles without failure. For steels, it is the stress level on the S-N curve where the curve becomes horizontal (asymptotic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fatigue Life (Nf):** The total number of stress cycles required to cause failure at a specified stress amplitude. It is read directly from the S-N (Stress–Number of cycles) curve, also called the Wöhler curve.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Paperclip Bending):** Take a standard steel paperclip and bend it 90° one way, then 90° the other way, repeatedly. The maximum stress during each bend is far below the steel's ultimate tensile strength, yet the clip eventually **snaps** at the bend point. This is **fatigue failure**. The clip "remembers" every cycle — micro-cracks initiate at the surface, propagate with each cycle, and finally fracture catastrophically without any warning. This is exactly what happens to aircraft wings, rotating shafts, bridges under wind loading, and railway axles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 (Bridge Wires):** A suspension bridge cable undergoes millions of tiny stress cycles due to wind gusts and traffic vibrations. Each cycle is harmless individually, but cumulatively, the wire fails after years of service. Engineers combat this by design rules that keep the working stress well below the **endurance limit** of the cable material.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Endurance Limit Ratio** for steels: **Se' = 0.5 × Sut** (for Sut ≤ 1400 MPa / 200 kpsi)
- **Standard Test Cycle:** Completely reversed rotating bending (R = −1) at ω ≈ 3000–10000 rpm
- **Standard Reference Endurance Limit:** At **10⁶ cycles** (for steels) and **5 × 10⁸ cycles** (for non-ferrous metals)
- **Fatigue Strength (Sf):** The stress amplitude at which failure occurs after a *specified* (finite) number of cycles.
- **Standard specimen:** R. R. Moore rotating-beam specimen (hourglass profile, gauge diameter ≈ 6–8 mm)

### 1.4 Why This Experiment Exists in the Lab

| Aspect | Static Test | Fatigue (Endurance) Test |
|---|---|---|
| Loading | Single, increasing load | Cyclic, fluctuating load |
| Failure stress | ≥ Yield / Ultimate | ≪ Yield strength |
| Failure mode | Bulk plastic deformation | Localized crack initiation & growth |
| Service relevance | Static structures | Shafts, springs, axles, gears, bridges |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Approximately **80%–90% of all mechanical service failures** in industry are attributed to fatigue. Hence, the endurance test is a cornerstone of design of machine elements (PCPEL308 directly supports the design-oriented course **PCPCT301 – Design of Machine Elements**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fatigue Phenomenon — Step-by-Step Logic

The fatigue process unfolds in **three distinct stages**:

1. **Crack Initiation (Stage I):**
   - Cyclic plastic deformation begins in the surface grains (or at inclusions, voids, or surface scratches).
   - Slip bands (called *persistent slip bands*, PSBs) extrude and intrude at the free surface.
   - Micro-cracks (≈ 1–10 µm length) nucleate along these PSBs.
   - Takes up to **~90% of total fatigue life** for long-life regimes.

2. **Crack Propagation (Stage II):**
   - One dominant micro-crack begins to grow perpendicular to the maximum tensile stress direction.
   - Each cycle advances the crack by a tiny increment `da/dN`.
   - Stage II is governed by the famous **Paris Law**: `da/dN = C(ΔK)^m`.
   - Striations (beach marks) become visible on the fracture surface.

3. **Final Fracture (Stage III):**
   - When the crack reaches a critical length, the remaining cross-section cannot support the load.
   - Sudden, brittle-like (often ductile) overload fracture occurs.
   - The fracture surface shows two distinct regions: **smooth fatigue zone** and **rough overload zone** (cup-and-cone or chevron marks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does fatigue happen below the yield strength?** In cyclic loading, localized micro-plastic strain occurs at stress concentrators (notches, grain boundaries) even when the nominal stress is well within the elastic range. Each cycle introduces irreversible micro-damage that accumulates.

### 2.2 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — Reading and Interpretation

- The horizontal axis is **N** (number of cycles, log scale).
- The vertical axis is **Sa** (stress amplitude, linear or log scale).
- For **ferrous metals and titanium**, the curve has a clear **knee** at **N = 10⁶** — below this, an endurance limit Se exists.
- For **non-ferrous metals** (Al, Cu, Mg), there is **no true endurance limit**; the curve continues to slope downward. A "**fatigue strength at 10⁸ or 5×10⁸ cycles**" is used as a design reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction:** Endurance Limit (true, infinite life) vs. Fatigue Strength (at finite specified life). Examiners love to test this distinction.

### 2.3 Basquin's Equation (High-Cycle Fatigue)

The linear portion of the S-N curve (in log-log axes) is described by:

$$S_f = a \cdot N_f^{b}$$

where `a` and `b` are material constants. It is more commonly written as:

$$S_f = S'_f \cdot (2N_f)^{b}$$

where `S'_f` is the **fatigue strength coefficient** (intercept at 2N = 1) and `b` is the **fatigue strength exponent** (slope, typically −0.05 to −0.12 for steels).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uncorrected Endurance Limit | $S_e' = 0.5 \cdot S_{ut}$ (Sut ≤ 1400 MPa) | MPa | Base estimate for steels |
| 2 | Corrected Endurance Limit | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \cdot S_e'$ | MPa | Real-machine design value |
| 3 | Surface Factor (machined/ground) | $k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^{b}$ (a=4.51, b=−0.265 for machined) | dimensionless | Accounts for surface finish |
| 4 | Size Factor (round, d in mm) | $k_b = 0.879 \cdot d^{-0.107}$ (for 2.79 ≤ d ≤ 51 mm) | dimensionless | Accounts for size effect |
| 5 | Load Factor | $k_c = 0.85$ (bending), $1.0$ (axial), $0.59$ (torsion) | dimensionless | Type of loading |
| 6 | Temperature Factor | $k_d$ from chart (≈ 1.0 below 50°C) | dimensionless | Operating temperature |
| 7 | Reliability Factor | $k_e = 0.814$ (90%), $0.753$ (95%), $0.659$ (99%) | dimensionless | Statistical scatter |
| 8 | Miscellaneous Factor | $k_f$ (≈ 1.0 default) | dimensionless | Residual stresses, etc. |
| 9 | Basquin's Equation | $S_f = S'_f (2N_f)^{b}$ | MPa | High-cycle fatigue life |
| 10 | Goodman Mean Stress Line | $\dfrac{S_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{S_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$ | dimensionless | Conservative design |
| 11 | Gerber Mean Stress Curve | $\left(\dfrac{S_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{S_a}{S_e} = 1$ | dimensionless | Less conservative |
| 12 | Soderberg Mean Stress Line | $\dfrac{S_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{S_m}{S_{y}} = 1$ | dimensionless | Most conservative (uses Sy) |
| 13 | Fatigue Stress Concentration | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | dimensionless | Notch sensitivity |
| 14 | Notch Sensitivity | $q = \dfrac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}}$ | dimensionless | Neuber's constant |
| 15 | Notch Strength | $\sigma_{notch} = S_e \cdot K_f$ | MPa | Notched endurance limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Reminder:** In KTU exams, students often confuse **alternating stress (Sa)**, **mean stress (Sm)**, and **maximum/minimum stresses (Smax, Smin)**. Memorize:
> $$S_m = \frac{S_{max} + S_{min}}{2}, \quad S_a = \frac{S_{max} - S_{min}}{2}$$

### 2.5 Stress Ratio (R) — Critical Parameter

$$R = \frac{S_{min}}{S_{max}}$$

| R value | Loading Type |
|---|---|
| R = −1 | Completely reversed (rotating-beam test) |
| R = 0 | Pulsating (zero to tension) |
| 0 < R < 1 | Tension-tension |
| R = 1 | Static |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Crankshaft, connecting rod, leaf spring, gear tooth design.
- **Aerospace:** Wing spar, fuselage skin, turbine blade (high-cycle fatigue + creep interaction).
- **Civil:** Bridge cables, pre-stressed concrete reinforcement.
- **Biomedical:** Orthopedic implants undergo ~10⁶ gait cycles per year — must be designed for *infinite life* below Se.
- **Power Generation:** Turbine shafts in thermal/nuclear plants operate 24×7 for decades — fatigue is a critical life-limiting factor.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Endurance Limit Modifying Equation

**Given:** Base material property is the rotating-beam endurance limit $S_e'$ (standard specimen under R = −1).

**Required:** Modified endurance limit $S_e$ for the actual component.

**Step 1 — Identify the standard test conditions:**
- Standard specimen: polished, gauge dia = 7.6 mm, R = −1, room temperature, 50% reliability.

**Step 2 — Each modifying factor multiplies the base value:**
- Surface finish reduces Se → $k_a < 1$
- Larger size increases stress gradient effect → $k_b < 1$
- Pure axial vs. bending changes volume under high stress → $k_c$
- High temperature reduces strength → $k_d < 1$
- Higher reliability demands lower allowable → $k_e < 1$

**Step 3 — Combine multiplicatively (independent factors):**

$$\boxed{S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'}$$

This is the Marin Equation (Joseph Marin, 1937) and is the **standard procedure** taught in KTU Design of Machine Elements.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Basquin's Equation from S-N Curve Data

**Setup:** Two known points on the linear (log-log) portion of the S-N curve.

- Point 1: At $N_1 = 10^{3}$ cycles, $S_{f1}$ is known (≈ 0.9 Sut)
- Point 2: At $N_2 = 10^{6}$ cycles, $S_{f2} = S_e'$

**Step 1 — Write Basquin's equation twice:**

$$S_{f1} = S'_f (2 N_1)^{b}$$
$$S_{f2} = S'_f (2 N_2)^{b}$$

**Step 2 — Divide the two equations to eliminate $S'_f$:**

$$\frac{S_{f1}}{S_{f2}} = \left(\frac{2 N_1}{2 N_2}\right)^{b} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^{b}$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural log of both sides:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{S_{f1}}{S_{f2}}\right) = b \cdot \ln\left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the exponent b:**

$$b = \frac{\ln(S_{f1}/S_{f2})}{\ln(N_1/N_2)}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the coefficient $S'_f$ using either point:**

$$S'_f = \frac{S_{f1}}{(2 N_1)^{b}}$$

**Step 6 — Use the result to predict life at any stress amplitude $S_f$:**

$$N_f = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{S_f}{S'_f}\right)^{1/b}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Question:** A steel component has $S_{ut} = 700$ MPa. The surface is machined. The component is 25 mm diameter, loaded in bending, at 50°C, and a reliability of 90% is required. The stress cycles between +300 MPa and −200 MPa. Calculate the factor of safety using the Goodman criterion.

**Step 1 — Compute the uncorrected endurance limit:**

$$S_e' = 0.5 \cdot S_{ut} = 0.5 \cdot 700 = 350 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute modifying factors:**

Surface factor (machined, Sut = 700 MPa):
$$k_a = 4.51 \cdot (700)^{-0.265}$$

Evaluating: $(700)^{-0.265} = e^{-0.265 \ln 700} = e^{-0.265 \cdot 6.551} = e^{-1.736} \approx 0.1763$

$$k_a = 4.51 \cdot 0.1763 \approx 0.795$$

Size factor (d = 25 mm):
$$k_b = 0.879 \cdot (25)^{-0.107} = 0.879 \cdot e^{-0.107 \cdot 3.219} = 0.879 \cdot e^{-0.3444} = 0.879 \cdot 0.7086 \approx 0.623$$

Load factor (bending): $k_c = 1.0$ (Note: For a non-rotating beam, some texts use 0.85; in KTU context with axial corrected, $k_c = 1.0$ is conventional). Let us use $k_c = 0.85$ for pure rotating bending component to be conservative.

Temperature factor: $k_d = 1.0$ (since 50°C is below the derating temperature)

Reliability factor: $k_e = 0.814$ (for 90%)

Miscellaneous factor: $k_f = 1.0$

**Step 3 — Compute the corrected endurance limit:**

$$S_e = 0.795 \cdot 0.623 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.814 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 350$$

$$S_e = 0.795 \cdot 0.623 = 0.4953$$
$$0.4953 \cdot 0.85 = 0.4210$$
$$0.4210 \cdot 0.814 = 0.3427$$
$$S_e = 0.3427 \cdot 350 \approx 119.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute mean and alternating stresses:**

$$S_m = \frac{S_{max} + S_{min}}{2} = \frac{300 + (-200)}{2} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

$$S_a = \frac{S_{max} - S_{min}}{2} = \frac{300 - (-200)}{2} = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Goodman criterion to find the allowable alternating stress:**

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{S_a}{S_e} + \frac{S_m}{S_{ut}}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{250}{119.9} + \frac{50}{700} = 2.085 + 0.0714 = 2.156$$

$$n = \frac{1}{2.156} \approx 0.464$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight:** Since $n < 1$, the component will **fail in fatigue**. The designer must either (a) increase the diameter, (b) improve the surface finish, (c) reduce the mean stress, or (d) use a higher-strength steel. KTU expects you to interpret the numerical result, not just compute it.

**Step 6 — Verify by computing required $S_e$ for $n = 1$:**

$$S_{e,req} = \frac{S_a \cdot S_{ut}}{S_{ut} - S_m} = \frac{250 \cdot 700}{700 - 50} = \frac{175000}{650} \approx 269.2 \text{ MPa}$$

So the component's actual $S_e$ (119.9 MPa) must be raised to at least 269.2 MPa, meaning the design is grossly under-spec.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Mean Stress Criteria

For the same $S_m = 50$ MPa, $S_{ut} = 700$ MPa, $S_e = 119.9$ MPa:

| Criterion | Equation | Allowable $S_a$ (MPa) | Factor of Safety n |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Goodman** | $S_a/119.9 + 50/700 = 1$ | $S_a = 119.9 \cdot (1 - 50/700) = 111.3$ | $n = 111.3/250 = 0.445$ |
| **Gerber** | $(50/700)^2 + S_a/119.9 = 1$ | $S_a = 119.9 \cdot (1 - 0.0051) = 119.3$ | $n = 119.3/250 = 0.477$ |
| **Soderberg** | $S_a/119.9 + 50/S_y = 1$ (assume $S_y = 0.7 \cdot 700 = 490$ MPa) | $S_a = 119.9 \cdot (1 - 50/490) = 107.7$ | $n = 107.7/250 = 0.431$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** For tensile mean stress, the **order of conservatism** is: **Soderberg > Goodman > Gerber**. KTU prefers **Goodman** for design (Shigley's textbook convention) unless specified otherwise.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Rotating-Beam Fatigue Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Supply 220V AC] --> B[Variable Frequency Drive]
    B --> C[High Speed Electric Motor]
    C --> D[Upper Spindle Chuck]
    D --> E[R.R. Moore Specimen Hourglass Shape]
    E --> F[Lower Spindle Chuck]
    F --> G[Ball Bearings Support]
    E --> H[Loading Lever with Dead Weights]
    H --> I[Static Bending Load Applied]
    I --> J[Cyclic Stress at Gauge Section]
    J --> K[Micro Crack Initiation]
    K --> L[Crack Propagation Stage II]
    L --> M[Final Fracture]
    M --> N[Cycle Counter Auto Stops]
    N --> O[Data Logger N and Sa]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The specimen is **stationary** while a **bending moment is applied** through a stationary dead-weight lever. As the specimen rotates, every fiber on its surface experiences fully reversed stress ($S_{max} = -S_{min}$), achieving R = −1 automatically.

---

### 4.2 S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — Schematic Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Stress Amplitude Sa on Y axis log] --> B[Cycle Count N on X axis log]
    B --> C{Steel or Non Ferrous}
    C --> D[Steel Sut le 1400 MPa]
    C --> E[Aluminum Copper Magnesium]
    D --> F[Sloping Line N = 10 to 10^6]
    F --> G[Knee at 10^6 cycles]
    G --> H[Horizontal Endurance Limit Se]
    H --> I[Safe Region Infinite Life]
    E --> J[Continuous Sloping Line]
    J --> K[No True Endurance Limit]
    K --> L[Use Fatigue Strength at 5x10^8]
```

---

### 4.3 The Three Stages of Fatigue Failure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cyclic Load Applied] --> B[Stage 1 Crack Initiation]
    B --> C[Stage 2 Crack Propagation Paris Law]
    C --> D[Stage 3 Final Fracture]
    B --> B1[Persistent Slip Bands at Surface]
    B --> B2[Micro Crack 1 to 10 micrometer]
    C --> C1[da per dN = C delta K power m]
    C --> C2[Beach Mark Striations Visible]
    D --> D1[Critical Crack Length Reached]
    D --> D2[Sudden Overload Fracture]
```

---

### 4.4 The Fatigue Test Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Step 1 Prepare Specimen] --> P2[Step 2 Measure Gauge Diameter d]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Polish Gauge Section]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Install in R.R. Moore Machine]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Select First Stress Level Sa1]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Apply Dead Weight]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Start Motor and Cycle Counter]
    P7 --> P8{Specimen Fractured}
    P8 -->|Yes| P9[Record N1 Cycles to Failure]
    P8 -->|No after 10^7 cycles| P10[Runout Mark as Endured]

    P10 --> P11
    P11 --> P12[Step 9 Plot S-N Curve]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sm on X axis] --> B[Sa on Y axis]
    B --> C[Static Failure Point Sut on X axis]
    C --> D[Yield Point Sy on X axis]
```
