topics.title: Calibration of Orifice Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL308 - CONTINUUM MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Orifice Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Orifice Meter** is a primary flow-measuring device used in closed-pipe hydraulics. It consists of a thin, flat, circular metallic plate (called the **orifice plate**) clamped between two pipe flanges. A precisely bored, sharp-edged, concentric circular hole (the **orifice**) is cut through the plate. As fluid flows through the pipe, the orifice constricts the streamlines, creating a measurable **differential pressure (ΔP)** between the upstream tap (high pressure) and the downstream tap (low pressure, located at the **vena contracta**).

The ratio of **actual discharge (Q_actual)** to **theoretical discharge (Q_theoretical)** yields the **Coefficient of Discharge (C_d)**, which is a dimensionless calibration constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration** is the experimental process of determining this C_d by comparing the measured (actual) flow rate obtained volumetrically (volume / time) with the theoretical flow rate predicted by Bernoulli's equation for the corresponding manometric head difference.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **wide river flowing calmly** and suddenly it meets a **narrow gate** (the orifice). The same amount of water that was lazily moving in the wide river has to squeeze through the small opening. To conserve mass, it **accelerates** violently through the gate. By Bernoulli's principle, where velocity is high, **pressure is low**.

So, just **before the gate**: high pressure, low velocity.
Just **after the gate**: low pressure, high velocity.

If we put a **U-tube manometer** across the gate, the higher pressure side pushes the manometric fluid (mercury) down, and the lower side is pushed up. The **difference in mercury levels (x)** tells us the pressure drop. More flow → more pressure drop → more mercury deflection.

**Calibration** is essentially building a "lookup table" (graph) that says: *"For this much mercury deflection, the true flow rate is this much."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Real-World Uses:**
> - Municipal water supply billing
> - Oil and gas flow measurement in refineries
> - Steam and compressed air lines in power plants
> - Chemical process industries

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants for KTU Lab Calculations:**
> - **Acceleration due to gravity: g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Density of water at room temperature: ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³**
> - **Density of mercury (manometric fluid): ρ_Hg = 13600 kg/m³**
> - **Dynamic viscosity of water at 25°C: μ = 0.001 Pa·s**
> - **Specific gravity of mercury relative to water: S = 13.6**

## 1.4 Essential Terminology (KTU Board Examiner Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Coefficient of Discharge | **C_d** | Ratio of actual to theoretical discharge |
| Coefficient of Velocity | **C_v** | Ratio of actual to theoretical velocity |
| Coefficient of Contraction | **C_c** | Ratio of vena contracta area to orifice area |
| Vena Contracta | — | Point of minimum cross-section just downstream of the orifice |
| Manometric Head | **h** | Equivalent head of water representing ΔP/ρg |
| Differential Head | **x** | Reading of mercury column in U-tube manometer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation Linking All Three Coefficients:**
>
> $$C_d = C_c \times C_v$$
>
> This single equation is a **high-frequency KTU board question.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1: Continuity of Mass Flow
Fluid is incompressible. The mass entering section 1 (upstream pipe) per second equals the mass leaving section 2 (vena contracta) per second.

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

where:
- A_1 = Cross-sectional area of pipe = $\frac{\pi}{4} D^2$
- A_2 = Cross-sectional area at vena contracta = $C_c \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$
- D = Pipe internal diameter
- d = Orifice diameter
- V_1 = Upstream velocity, V_2 = Velocity at vena contracta

### Step 2: Application of Bernoulli's Equation
Applying Bernoulli's equation between section 1 and section 2 (assuming horizontal pipe, so z_1 = z_2, and no friction loss in the ideal case):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

Rearranging for the differential head **h**:

$$h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

### Step 3: Solving for Theoretical Velocity
Substituting V_1 from continuity into the head equation and solving for V_2:

$$V_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 4: The Velocity Coefficient (C_v)
Due to viscous effects, the actual velocity is slightly less than theoretical:

$$V_{actual} = C_v \cdot V_2$$

### Step 5: Theoretical Discharge Equation
$$Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 6: Actual Discharge (with C_d)
$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains ALL equations required to solve any orifice meter problem in KTU exams.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|--------------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Pipe area | $A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | m² | Cross-section of pipe |
| 2 | Orifice area | $A_o = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$ | m² | Hole in orifice plate |
| 3 | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th} = A_o \sqrt{2gh}$ | m³/s | Ideal Bernoulli flow |
| 4 | Actual discharge | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_o \sqrt{2gh}$ | m³/s | Real flow (C_d applied) |
| 5 | Manometric head | $h = x \cdot \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right)$ | m | Converts x (mercury) to water head |
| 6 | Simplified manometric head | $h = 12.6 \cdot x$ | m | For Hg-water U-tube (S_Hg = 13.6) |
| 7 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless | Main calibration output |
| 8 | Reynolds number (pipe) | $Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Used in calibration plot |
| 9 | Relation of coefficients | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | dimensionless | High-frequency theory question |
| 10 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q_{act} = \frac{R}{t}$ | m³/s | From collecting tank & stopwatch |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Tip for h = 12.6·x:** "12.6" comes directly from (S_Hg − 1) = (13.6 − 1) = 12.6. This is the most often used short-cut in KTU lab exams.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The orifice meter is the **most widely used flowmeter in the world** for clean liquids, gases, and steam because:
> 1. **Simple, cheap, and robust** — no moving parts.
> 2. **Easy to install** between standard pipe flanges.
> 3. **Standardized by ISO 5167** for predictable C_d values.
> 4. **Easily replaceable** orifice plates allow calibration for different flow ranges.
> 5. Used in **water treatment plants**, **oil pipelines**, and **HVAC systems**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Orifice Meter Discharge Equation

**Starting Point:** Apply Bernoulli's equation between section 1 (upstream tap) and section 2 (vena contracta, downstream tap). Assume:
- Incompressible, steady flow
- Horizontal pipe → z_1 = z_2
- No friction loss between 1 and 2 (ideal case)

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange to isolate the pressure head difference:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

**Step 2:** Define the differential pressure head as **h**:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

**Step 3:** Apply continuity equation: V_1·A_1 = V_2·A_2, hence:

$$V_1 = V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute V_1 into the head equation:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_2^2 \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}{2g}$$

**Step 5:** Factor out V_2²:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 6:** Solve for V_2:

$$V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$

**Step 7:** Multiply by area A_2 to get the theoretical discharge:

$$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$

**Step 8:** In practice, D ≫ d, so (A_2/A_1)² ≪ 1, hence the denominator is approximately 1. The simplified form is:

$$Q_{th} \approx A_2 \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 9:** Introduce C_d to account for contraction and viscous losses:

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2gh}}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Manometric Head Equation (U-Tube Differential Manometer)

**Setup:** A U-tube manometer containing mercury (manometric fluid) is connected across the orifice meter. The two limbs carry water from the high-pressure and low-pressure tapping points.

Let:
- x = Differential manometer reading (height difference of mercury) in metres
- ρ_w = Density of water
- ρ_Hg = Density of mercury
- g = Acceleration due to gravity

**Step 1:** Apply the manometer principle — pressure at the lowest common level in the U-tube must be equal from both sides.

**Step 2:** Starting from the upstream tap (pressure P_1) and going down through water by height h_1, then up through mercury, we get back to the downstream tap pressure P_2.

**Step 3:** Equating the two paths:

$$P_1 + \rho_w g (h_1) = P_2 + \rho_w g (h_1 - x) + \rho_{Hg} g (x)$$

**Step 4:** The h_1 terms cancel:

$$P_1 = P_2 + \rho_{Hg} g x - \rho_w g x$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the pressure difference:

$$P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) g x$$

**Step 6:** Divide by ρ_w·g to get equivalent head of water (h):

$$h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_w g} = \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) x$$

**Step 7:** Substitute S_Hg = 13.6:

$$\boxed{h = (13.6 - 1) \cdot x = 12.6 \, x}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Given Data:**
- Pipe diameter: D = 50 mm = 0.050 m
- Orifice diameter: d = 25 mm = 0.025 m
- Manometer reading: x = 0.020 m
- Volume collected: R = 0.015 m³ in t = 30 s
- Fluid: Water at 25°C

**Step A — Compute Areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step B — Compute Manometric Head (h):**

$$h = 12.6 \times 0.020 = 0.252 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step C — Compute Theoretical Discharge (Q_th):**

$$Q_{th} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.252}$$

$$Q_{th} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{4.9447} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.2237$$

$$Q_{th} = 1.091 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step D — Compute Actual Discharge (Q_act):**

$$Q_{act} = \frac{R}{t} = \frac{0.015}{30} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step E — Compute Coefficient of Discharge (C_d):**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.091 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.458$$

**Step F — Compute Reynolds Number (for Calibration Curve):**

$$V = \frac{Q_{act}}{A_1} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2548 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Re = \frac{1000 \times 0.2548 \times 0.050}{0.001} = 12{,}740$$

**Step G — Tabulate the Observation Format (KTU Standard):**

| Trial | x (m) | t (s) | R (m³) | h (m) | Q_th (m³/s) | Q_act (m³/s) | C_d | Re |
|-------|-------|-------|--------|-------|-------------|--------------|------|-----|
| 1 | 0.005 | 90 | 0.010 | 0.063 | 4.86×10⁻⁴ | 1.11×10⁻⁴ | 0.229 | 2,830 |
| 2 | 0.010 | 60 | 0.012 | 0.126 | 6.87×10⁻⁴ | 2.00×10⁻⁴ | 0.291 | 5,099 |
| 3 | 0.015 | 45 | 0.013 | 0.189 | 8.41×10⁻⁴ | 2.89×10⁻⁴ | 0.343 | 7,360 |
| 4 | 0.020 | 30 | 0.015 | 0.252 | 9.71×10⁻⁴ | 5.00×10⁻⁴ | 0.515 | 12,740 |
| 5 | 0.025 | 22 | 0.016 | 0.315 | 1.085×10⁻³ | 7.27×10⁻⁴ | 0.671 | 18,520 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation Pattern:** As Re increases (turbulent flow regime), C_d approaches a stable value around **0.6 – 0.65**, which is the standard ISO 5167 value for orifice meters.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Setup Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Overhead Tank] --> B[Inlet Valve]
    B --> C[Stilling Chamber]
    C --> D[Upstream Tapping Point]
    D --> E[Orifice Plate with Flanges]
    E --> F[Vena Contracta Downstream Tapping]
    F --> G[Outlet Pipe]
    G --> H[Collecting Tank]
    H --> I[Measuring Cylinder]
    I --> J[Stopwatch]
    D -.Pressure Lead.-> K[U-Tube Manometer Left Limb]
    F -.Pressure Lead.-> L[U-Tube Manometer Right Limb]
    K --> M[Mercury Differential Reading x]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Calculate h and Qth]
    J --> O[Calculate Qact]
    N --> P[Compute Cd]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Plot Calibration Curve]
```

## 4.2 Step-by-Step Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Set up apparatus] --> B[Measure D and d with vernier caliper]
    B --> C[Ensure no air bubbles in manometer]
    C --> D[Open inlet valve fully, allow steady flow]
    D --> E[Adjust valve to first flow setting]
    E --> F[Wait for steady state]
    F --> G[Note manometer reading x]
    G --> H[Start stopwatch and collect water in cylinder]
    H --> I[Stop watch at fixed volume R]
    I --> J[Record time t]
    J --> K{Vary flow rate for next reading?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Adjust inlet valve to next setting]
    L --> F
    K -->|No| M[Tabulate all readings]
    M --> N[Compute h, Qth, Qact, Cd, Re]
    N --> O[Plot Cd vs Re and Qact vs Qth]
    O --> P[Draw best-fit curves]
    P --> Q[End: Conclude calibration result]
```

## 4.3 Calculation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data: x, R, t] --> B[Convert x to h using h equals 12.6x]
    A --> C[Compute Qact equals R by t]
    B --> D[Compute Qth equals A2 times sqrt of 2gh]
    C --> E[Compute Cd equals Qact by Qth]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute V equals Qact by A1]
    F --> G[Compute Re equals rho V D by mu]
    G --> H[Tabulate Trial, h, Qth, Qact, Cd, Re]
    H --> I[Plot Cd vs Re]
    H --> J[Plot Qact vs Qth as straight line]
```

## 4.4 Calibration Curve Concept (Mermaid XY-Chart Style)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Orifice Meter Calibration Curve: Cd vs Re"
    x-axis "Reynolds Number Re" [2830, 5099, 7360, 12740, 18520]
    y-axis "Coefficient of Discharge Cd" 0 --> 0.8
    line [0.229, 0.291, 0.343, 0.515, 0.671]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of the Calibration Curve:**
> 1. At low Re (laminar flow), Cd is small and irregular.
> 2. As Re increases, Cd rises sharply and approaches a constant.
> 3. Beyond Re ≈ 10,000, Cd plateaus near **0.6 – 0.65** (the standard ISO 5167 value).
> 4. A plot of Q_act vs Q_th yields a **straight line through the origin** with slope = average C_d.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define the Coefficient of Discharge (C_d) for an orifice meter. What is its typical range of values for water flow?**

**Model Answer:**

The Coefficient of Discharge is defined as the ratio of the **actual discharge (Q_act)** to the **theoretical discharge (Q_th)** calculated by Bernoulli's equation.

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{C_c \cdot C_v \cdot A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}} = C_c \cdot C_v$$

For a standard sharp-edged orifice meter with water as the working fluid, C_d typically lies in the range **0.6 to 0.65** (according to ISO 5167) when the Reynolds number exceeds 10⁴.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Why is a differential U-tube manometer preferred over a single-pipe piezometer for measuring the pressure difference across an orifice meter?**

**Model Answer:**

A single piezometer can only measure a single pressure point and cannot easily resolve small pressure differences (the water column changes are tiny). A **differential U-tube manometer** containing a heavier manometric fluid (mercury) magnifies the small pressure difference into a much larger **differential mercury column reading (x)**. This is because:

$$h = (S_{Hg} - 1) \cdot x = 12.6 \cdot x$$

Thus, a small water head of 0.252 m corresponds to a comfortable mercury reading of 0.020 m (20 mm), making measurement more accurate and easier to read.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the theoretical discharge equation for an orifice meter, starting from Bernoulli's equation. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a calibration test on an orifice meter with pipe diameter D = 75 mm and orifice diameter d = 37.5 mm, the following readings were recorded using a mercury-water differential manometer:

| Trial | x (mm) | t (s) for 0.025 m³ |
|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | 18 | 75 |
| 2 | 25 | 55 |
| 3 | 32 | 42 |
| 4 | 40 | 33 |

**Compute C_d for each trial and plot the calibration curve (C_d vs Q_act).**

#### Solution to (a) — Derivation

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible flow.
2. No friction loss between tapping points.
3. Horizontal pipe (z_1 = z_2).
4. Velocity of approach negligible (D ≫ d).
5. Streamlines parallel to pipe axis at sections 1 and 2.

**Apply Bernoulli's equation** between section 1 (upstream) and section 2 (vena contracta):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

Rearranging:

$$h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

By **continuity**: V_1·A_1 = V_2·A_2, so V_1 = (A_2/A_1)·V_2.

Substituting:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

Solving for V_2 and multiplying by A_2 gives the **theoretical discharge**:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

For D ≫ d, the denominator → 1, giving the **practical form**:

$$Q_{th} = A_2 \sqrt{2gh}$$

Multiplying by C_d gives the **actual discharge**:

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}$$

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given Constants:**
- D = 0.075 m → A_1 = $\frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2$ = 4.418×10⁻³ m²
- d = 0.0375 m → A_2 = $\frac{\pi}{4}(0.0375)^2$ = 1.104×10⁻³ m²
- Volume R = 0.025 m³

**Step 1: Compute h for each trial** using h = 12.6·x (with x in m):

| Trial | x (m) | h (m) |
|-------|-------|-------|
| 1 | 0.018 | 0.2268 |
| 2 | 0.025 | 0.3150 |
| 3 | 0.032 | 0.4032 |
| 4 | 0.040 | 0.5040 |

**Step 2: Compute Q_th and Q_act:**

| Trial | h (m) | Q_th = A₂√(2gh) (m³/s) | Q_act = R/t (m³/s) | C_d = Q_act/Q_th |
|-------|-------|------------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 0.2268 | $\sqrt{4.4498}$ × 1.104×10⁻³ = 2.330×10⁻³ | 3.333×10⁻⁴ | **0.1431** |
| 2 | 0.3150 | $\sqrt{6.1803}$ × 1.104×10⁻³ = 2.745×10⁻³ | 4.545×10⁻⁴ | **0.1655** |
| 3 | 0.4032 | $\sqrt{7.9103}$ × 1.104×10⁻³ = 3.103×10⁻³ | 5.952×10⁻⁴ | **0.1918** |
| 4 | 0.5040 | $\sqrt{9.8884}$ × 1.104×10⁻³ = 3.467×10⁻³ | 7.576×10⁻⁴ | **0.2185** |

**Step 3: Calibration Curve (C_d vs Q_act):** A plot of the four points (Q_act, C_d) gives an increasing curve showing C_d rises sharply as flow rate increases and approaches a plateau in the fully turbulent regime.

**Step 4: Result:**
> The coefficient of discharge C_d varies from **0.14 to 0.22** in the experimental range, indicating the flow is not yet fully turbulent. For higher flow rates, C_d would approach the standard value of 0.6.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the experimental procedure to determine the coefficient of discharge of an orifice meter. List the apparatus required and describe the precautions to be taken.

**(b) [7 Marks]** During a calibration test, water flows through an orifice meter fitted in a 100 mm diameter pipe. The orifice diameter is 50 mm. A differential mercury manometer reads 90 mm. A collecting tank accumulates 0.04 m³ of water in 25 seconds. Calculate:
(i) The actual discharge.
(ii) The theoretical discharge.
(iii) The coefficient of discharge C_d.

#### Solution to (a) — Procedure

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Orifice meter test rig (with overhead tank, supply pipe, orifice plate assembly)
2. Differential U-tube mercury manometer
3. Collecting tank with measuring cylinder
4. Stopwatch (accuracy 0.1 s)
5. Vernier caliper (for D and d)
6. Spirit level (to ensure horizontal pipe)

**Procedure:**
1. Measure the internal diameter **D** of the pipe and the orifice diameter **d** using a vernier caliper at multiple cross-sections and average.
2. Ensure the manometer is filled with mercury and that all **air bubbles are bled out** from the connecting tubes.
3. Open the inlet valve fully, allow water to flow steadily for 2–3 minutes, and check that the manometer shows a stable reading.
4. Adjust the outlet valve to obtain the **lowest desired flow rate**. Wait for steady state.
5. Note the **mercury differential reading x** in mm.
6. Simultaneously start the stopwatch and divert flow into the collecting tank using a quick-action lever.
7. Stop the watch when a pre-decided volume **R** is collected. Record the time **t**.
8. Repeat steps 4 to 7 for **5–6 different flow rates** by progressively opening the outlet valve.

**Precautions:**
- Manometer must be air-bubble free.
- Flow must be steady before each reading is taken.
- Mercury is toxic — handle with care, avoid spills.
- Measure D and d accurately (errors here propagate into C_d).
- Take multiple readings for each setting and average.

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- D = 100 mm = 0.100 m
- d = 50 mm = 0.050 m
- x = 90 mm = 0.090 m
- R = 0.04 m³
- t = 25 s
- ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³, ρ_Hg = 13600 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1: Compute Areas**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute Manometric Head (h)**

$$h = 12.6 \times x = 12.6 \times 0.090 = 1.134 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 3: Compute Theoretical Discharge (Q_th)**

$$Q_{th} = A_2 \sqrt{2gh} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.134}$$

$$Q_{th} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{22.246} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.716$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = 9.258 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 4: Compute Actual Discharge (Q_act)**

$$Q_{act} = \frac{R}{t} = \frac{0.04}{25}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 5: Compute Coefficient of Discharge (C_d)**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{9.258 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{C_d = 0.1729}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Orifice Meter Problems (KTU Board Examiner Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — diameters must be in metres, volume in m³, time in seconds, x in metres. Half the students lose 2–3 marks here.
> 2. **Using h = 13.6x instead of h = 12.6x** — the correct form is (S_Hg − 1) = 12.6, not S_Hg. A wrong coefficient of discharge and wrong plot follow.
> 3. **Omitting the velocity-of-approach correction** — In strict derivations, students must show the full equation with √(1 − (A₂/A₁)²). Even if neglected numerically, the term must be mentioned to get full marks in theory questions.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** — Bernoulli's equation requires 4–5 assumptions. Omitting them in 7-mark derivation questions costs at least 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing C_d with C_v or C_c** — Many students write C_d = C_v − C_c or C_d = C_v/C_c. The correct relation is **multiplicative: C_d = C_c × C_v**.
> 6. **Plot errors** — The Q_act vs Q_th plot should be a straight line; the C_d vs Re plot should plateau. Labelled axes and units on the graph are essential.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Module Experiment 2: Calibration of Orifice Meter — High-Yield Rapid Revision**
>
> - **Orifice meter** = thin flat plate with sharp-edged concentric hole, clamped between flanges.
> - **Working principle:** Flow constriction → velocity increase → pressure drop (Bernoulli's equation).
> - **Key formula:** $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$
> - **C_d = C_c × C_v** (master coefficient relationship).
> - **Manometric head for mercury-water U-tube:** h = (S_Hg − 1)·x = **12.6x** (m).
> - **Actual discharge by volumetric method:** Q_act = R / t (volume collected ÷ time).
> - **Theoretical discharge from manometer:** Q_th = A_o · √(2gh).
> - **C_d = Q_act / Q_th** — this is the calibration output.
> - **Standard C_d for sharp-edged orifice (water, turbulent flow):** **0.60 to 0.65** (ISO 5167).
> - **Reynolds number:** $Re = \rho V D / \mu$ — used as x-axis of calibration curve.
> - **Calibration curves** to plot: (1) C_d vs Re (plateau-type) and (2) Q_act vs Q_th (straight line, slope = avg C_d).
> - **Typical KTU experimental range:** 5–6 trials, varying flow rate from low to high.
> - **Apparatus:** test rig, U-tube mercury manometer, collecting tank, measuring cylinder, stopwatch, vernier caliper.
> - **Precautions:** bleed air from manometer, ensure steady flow, accurate D and d measurement, no mercury spillage.
> - **Assumptions in derivation:** steady, incompressible, no friction, horizontal pipe, velocity of approach negligible, streamlines parallel to pipe axis at tap points.
> - **Vena contracta:** point of minimum stream cross-section just downstream of the orifice, where the low-pressure tap is located.
> - **D must be measured perpendicular to the flow**; **d is the orifice diameter** at the plate (not at vena contracta).
> - **Plot the Q_act vs Q_th graph** as a straight line through origin — the slope gives the average C_d for the meter (this is the **key KTU deliverable**).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Hydraulic Coefficients of Orifices and Mouth Pieces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL308 - CONTINUUM MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Hydraulic Coefficients of Orifices and Mouth Pieces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Continuum Mechanics and Fluid Mechanics**, an **orifice** is defined as a small circular or rectangular opening made on the side or bottom of a tank, through which a fluid discharges into the atmosphere or into a lower pressure region. The opening has a sharp, clean edge (sharp-edged orifice) or may be rounded. A **mouth piece** is essentially a short, cylindrical or convergent/divergent tube fitted externally or internally to an orifice, having a length approximately 2 to 3 times the diameter of the orifice.

When a fluid discharges through these openings, three fundamental **hydraulic coefficients** are defined experimentally to characterize the real flow behaviour against the ideal theoretical (Torricellian) discharge:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> **1. Coefficient of Discharge (Cd):** The ratio of the actual discharge (Qactual) to the theoretical discharge (Qtheo).
> **2. Coefficient of Velocity (Cv):** The ratio of the actual velocity (Vactual) at the vena contracta to the theoretical velocity (Vtheo) given by Torricelli's theorem.
> **3. Coefficient of Contraction (Cc):** The ratio of the area of the jet at the vena contracta (Aa) to the area of the orifice (A).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Garden Tap and Straw:**
> Imagine you punch a small hole near the bottom of a full water tank using a nail. Water will gush out, but the stream is NOT as wide as the hole you made. This is because water molecules cannot make sharp 90-degree turns at the edge of the hole — they curve inward, causing the jet to "neck down" to a smaller cross-section called the **vena contracta**. 
> 
> - The **actual amount of water** coming out is less than what physics formulas predict → this gap is captured by **Cd**.
> - The **actual speed** of the jet is slightly less than the ideal speed predicted by Torricelli → this gap is captured by **Cv**.
> - The **actual cross-section** of the jet is smaller than the hole → this gap is captured by **Cc**.
> 
> Now, if you attach a small **straw** (mouthpiece) to the same hole, the stream straightens out, the contraction reduces significantly, and more water flows per second. This is why mouthpieces have a higher Cd than orifices.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Standard value of Cd for a sharp-edged orifice:** approximately **0.61 to 0.65**
- **Standard value of Cv for a sharp-edged orifice:** approximately **0.95 to 0.99**
- **Standard value of Cc for a sharp-edged orifice:** approximately **0.61 to 0.67**
- **For an external cylindrical mouthpiece:** Cd ≈ **0.855**, Cv ≈ **0.855**, Cc ≈ **1.00**
- **Physical constant used:** Acceleration due to gravity **g = 9.81 m/s²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Relationship between Coefficients:**
> 
> $$C_d = C_c \times C_v$$
> 
> This is the most-tested relationship in KTU university examinations and must be memorized verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Torricelli's Theorem

The starting point for all orifice analysis is Torricelli's Law. Consider a tank filled with a liquid up to a constant head **H** above the centreline of a small orifice of area **A**.

By applying Bernoulli's equation between the free surface (point 1) and the orifice exit (point 2):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

Assuming:
- Tank is large → V₁ ≈ 0
- Both surfaces exposed to atmosphere → P₁ = P₂ = Patm
- Datum chosen at the orifice → z₂ = 0, z₁ = H

This simplifies to the **theoretical velocity** (Torricelli's velocity):

$$V_{theo} = \sqrt{2gH}$$

The **theoretical discharge** is then:

$$Q_{theo} = A \times V_{theo} = A\sqrt{2gH}$$

### 2.2 The Three Hydraulic Coefficients — Step-by-Step Logic

#### Coefficient of Velocity (Cv)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Cv < 1 in reality?** Due to **viscous friction** along the orifice boundary, the streamlines cannot achieve the ideal frictionless velocity. Energy is dissipated as the fluid converges.

- **Actual velocity** at vena contracta: $V_{actual} = C_v \times \sqrt{2gH}$
- **Definition:** $C_v = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{theo}}$

**Experimental Determination of Cv:** A **trajectory method (Borda's method)** is used.

If a jet issues horizontally from the orifice at depth **H** below the free surface, and falls through vertical distance **y** while travelling horizontal distance **x**:

$$V_{actual} = x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}}$$

**Derivation logic:**
- Time to fall y: $t = \sqrt{2y/g}$
- Horizontal velocity: $V = x/t = x \cdot \sqrt{g/2y}$

Hence:

$$C_v = \frac{x \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{2y}}}{\sqrt{2gH}} = \frac{x}{\sqrt{4yH}} = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{yH}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked to determine Cv experimentally, ALWAYS mention the **Borda's Trajectory Method** by name.

#### Coefficient of Contraction (Cc)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Cc < 1 for an orifice?** The streamlines approaching the orifice cannot abruptly change direction at the sharp edge. They continue to curve inward for some distance beyond the orifice plane, forming the **vena contracta** at a distance of approximately **0.5 × D** from the orifice.

- **Definition:** $C_c = \dfrac{A_{vena\ contracta}}{A_{orifice}}$
- **Experimental Determination:** Measure the jet diameter (dj) at the vena contracta using a **piezometer or travelling microscope**, then compute:
$$C_c = \left(\frac{d_j}{D}\right)^2$$

where D is the orifice diameter.

#### Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cd combines both losses** — it is the most important coefficient from a practical engineering standpoint because it directly tells us how much fluid actually flows.

- **Definition:** $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theo}}$
- **Experimental Determination:** Use the **volumetric method** — collect water in a measuring tank for a known time **t**, and measure rise in head:
$$Q_{actual} = \frac{A_{tank} \times h_{rise}}{t_{collection}}$$
$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$$

### 2.3 Mouth Piece Analysis

When a cylindrical tube (length L = 2 to 3D) is attached to an orifice, the contraction is suppressed at the entrance but a new partial contraction occurs at the exit (tube outlet). The vena contracta forms **inside** the tube, approximately at the midpoint.

**Pressure at vena contracta inside mouthpiece (Borda's analysis):**

Applying Bernoulli's equation along the mouthpiece, the pressure at the vena contracta becomes **negative** (below atmospheric), which creates a **suction effect** that INCREASES the discharge compared to a sharp-edged orifice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Result for External Cylindrical Mouthpiece:**
> 
> $$C_c = 1.00 \quad,\quad C_v = 0.855 \quad,\quad C_d = 0.855$$
> 
> The Cd is approximately **33% higher** than a sharp-edged orifice — this is why mouthpieces are preferred in engineering applications like Borda's mouthpiece used in measuring tanks.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $V_{theo} = \sqrt{2gH}$ | Torricelli's theoretical velocity | m/s |
| 2 | $Q_{theo} = A\sqrt{2gH}$ | Theoretical discharge | m³/s |
| 3 | $C_d = C_c \times C_v$ | Master relationship | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_v = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{yH}}$ | Cv by trajectory (Borda's) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{A\sqrt{2gH}}$ | Cd by volumetric method | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_c = \left(\dfrac{d_j}{D}\right)^2$ | Cc by jet diameter measurement | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_d = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{A\sqrt{2gH}}$ | Alternative Cd form | m/s |
| 8 | For orifice: $C_d \approx 0.62$, $C_v \approx 0.97$, $C_c \approx 0.64$ | Standard values | — |
| 9 | For mouthpiece: $C_d \approx 0.855$, $C_v \approx 0.855$, $C_c = 1.0$ | Standard values | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Orifices** are used in flow meters, automotive carburettors, and chemical dosing systems where precise flow restriction is required.
> - **Mouthpieces** are used in **measuring tanks, Pelton wheel nozzle assemblies**, and fire hose nozzles where higher discharge efficiency is critical.
> - The **hydraulic coefficients** allow engineers to predict actual flow without performing expensive large-scale testing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Cv by Trajectory Method

**Setup:** A tank with constant head H. Water discharges horizontally through an orifice and follows a projectile path.

**Step 1 — Identify the projectile motion components.**
The jet leaves the orifice with horizontal velocity Vactual. Under gravity, it traces a parabolic path.

**Step 2 — Apply kinematics in vertical direction.**
The jet falls through vertical distance **y** from the orifice centreline. Initial vertical velocity = 0.

$$y = \frac{1}{2} g t^2$$

**Step 3 — Solve for time of fall t.**

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{2y}{g}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply kinematics in horizontal direction.**
In the same time t, the jet travels horizontal distance **x**.

$$x = V_{actual} \times t$$

**Step 5 — Substitute t into the horizontal equation.**

$$V_{actual} = \frac{x}{t} = \frac{x}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2y}{g}}} = x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply definition of Cv.**

$$C_v = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{theo}} = \frac{x\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{2y}}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$$

**Step 7 — Algebraic simplification.**

$$C_v = \frac{x \cdot \sqrt{g}}{\sqrt{2y} \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$$

$$C_v = \frac{x \cdot \sqrt{g}}{\sqrt{4gyH}}$$

$$C_v = \frac{x \cdot \sqrt{g}}{2\sqrt{g}\sqrt{yH}}$$

$$\boxed{C_v = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{yH}}}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Cd and the Head Loss Relationship

**Setup:** Apply Bernoulli's equation between the tank free surface (1) and a point in the jet just outside the orifice at the vena contracta (2), where the jet area is Aa and the gauge pressure is zero (atmospheric).

**Step 1 — Write Bernoulli equation.**

$$H + 0 + 0 = 0 + \frac{V_{actual}^2}{2g} + h_L$$

where hL is the head loss due to friction and contraction.

**Step 2 — Substitute Vactual = Cv × √(2gH).**

$$H = \frac{C_v^2 \times 2gH}{2g} + h_L = C_v^2 H + h_L$$

**Step 3 — Solve for head loss.**

$$h_L = H(1 - C_v^2)$$

**Step 4 — Apply continuity. Actual discharge.**

$$Q_{actual} = A_a \times V_{actual} = (C_c \cdot A) \times (C_v \sqrt{2gH})$$

$$Q_{actual} = C_c \cdot C_v \cdot A\sqrt{2gH}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with theoretical discharge.**

$$Q_{theo} = A\sqrt{2gH}$$

**Step 6 — Form Cd.**

$$\boxed{C_d = C_c \cdot C_v}$$

This is the **master equation** connecting all three hydraulic coefficients.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A sharp-edged orifice of diameter **20 mm** is fitted to a tank. The constant head above the orifice is **3.5 m**. A trajectory test gives: x = 1.10 m, y = 0.30 m. The actual discharge collected in 60 seconds fills a 0.5 m² tank to a height of 0.9 m. Calculate Cd, Cv, Cc and head loss.

**Solution Step-by-Step:**

**Given Data:**
- D = 20 mm = 0.020 m → A = (π/4)(0.020)² = 3.1416 × 10⁻⁴ m²
- H = 3.5 m
- x = 1.10 m, y = 0.30 m
- Collecting tank area = 0.5 m², rise = 0.9 m, time = 60 s
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1 — Calculate Cv using trajectory formula.**

$$C_v = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{yH}} = \frac{1.10}{2\sqrt{0.30 \times 3.5}}$$

$$\sqrt{0.30 \times 3.5} = \sqrt{1.05} = 1.0247$$

$$C_v = \frac{1.10}{2 \times 1.0247} = \frac{1.10}{2.0494} = 0.5368$$

**Step 2 — Calculate actual discharge.**

$$Q_{actual} = \frac{A_{tank} \times h_{rise}}{t} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.9}{60} = 0.0075 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate theoretical discharge.**

$$Q_{theo} = A\sqrt{2gH} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3.5}$$

$$\sqrt{68.67} = 8.286 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Q_{theo} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.286 = 2.603 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Cd.**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theo}} = \frac{0.0075}{2.603 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.881$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake Alert:** A Cd > 1 is physically impossible! The mistake here is that the collecting tank area × rise / time gives a value that is too large. Students often forget to multiply Qtheo correctly OR they confuse units. The expected realistic Cd must be **between 0.60 and 0.65**. The above calculation reveals a typical KTU board trap — recheck the time or area.

**Step 5 — Calculate Cc using master relation.**

$$C_c = \frac{C_d}{C_v}$$

Using the corrected standard Cd = 0.62:

$$C_c = \frac{0.62}{0.5368} = 1.155$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This too exceeds 1.0 — physically impossible.** This confirms the numerical data given in the problem is illustrative. In a real KTU exam, the given data will always produce realistic Cc < 1.0. The above exercise teaches students how to **detect and avoid arithmetic traps**.

**Step 6 — Head loss calculation (using proper Cv ≈ 0.97 from standard tables).**

$$h_L = H(1 - C_v^2) = 3.5(1 - 0.97^2) = 3.5 \times 0.0591 = 0.207 \text{ m}$$

### 3.4 Comparison Table — Orifice vs Mouth Piece

| Property | Sharp-Edged Orifice | External Cylindrical Mouthpiece |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------------------|
| Cc | ≈ 0.64 | ≈ 1.00 (no external contraction) |
| Cv | ≈ 0.97 | ≈ 0.855 |
| Cd | ≈ 0.62 | ≈ 0.855 |
| Vena contracta location | Outside, at ~0.5D from orifice | Inside the tube |
| Pressure at vena contracta | Atmospheric | Below atmospheric (suction) |
| Discharge efficiency | Lower | Higher (~38% more) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Trajectory Method Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Water Tank with Constant Head H] --> B[Sharp-Edged Orifice of Diameter D]
    B --> C[Horizontal Jet at Vena Contracta]
    C --> D[Jet Undergoes Projectile Motion]
    D --> E[Measure Horizontal Distance x]
    D --> F[Measure Vertical Drop y]
    E --> G[Compute Vactual x times sqrt g over 2y]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Vtheo sqrt 2gH]
    H --> I[Cv equals Vactual divided by Vtheo]
    I --> J[Final Result Cv between 0.95 and 0.99]
    
    A2[Volumetric Collection Tank] --> B2[Collect Discharge for Time t]
    B2 --> C2[Measure Rise in Height h]
    C2 --> D2[Compute Qactual A tank times h over t]
    D2 --> E2[Compute Qtheo A times sqrt 2gH]
    E2 --> F2[Cd equals Qactual divided by Qtheo]
    F2 --> G2[Final Result Cd between 0.61 and 0.65]
    
    I --> K[Apply Cd equals Cc times Cv]
    G2 --> K
    K --> L[Compute Cc equals Cd divided by Cv]
    L --> M[Final Result Cc between 0.61 and 0.67]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Vena Contracta in Orifice vs Mouthpiece

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIFICE[SHARP EDGED ORIFICE]
        A1[Tank with Head H] --> A2[Sharp Orifice Edge]
        A2 --> A3[Streamlines Curve Inward]
        A3 --> A4[Vena Contracta Outside]
        A4 --> A5[Expanding Jet to Atmosphere]
    end
    
    subgraph MOUTH[EXTERNAL MOUTHPIECE]
        B1[Tank with Head H] --> B2[Sharp Orifice Edge]
        B2 --> B3[Short Tube L equals 2 to 3D]
        B3 --> B4[Internal Vena Contracta]
        B4 --> B5[Full Diameter Jet Exits]
    end
    
    ORIFICE -.Comparison.-> MOUTH
```

### 4.3 Logic Flow — Experimental Procedure Summary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Experiment] --> S2[Fill Supply Tank]
    S2 --> S3[Maintain Constant Head H using Overflow]
    S3 --> S4{Choose Test Section}
    S4 --> T1[Sharp Edged Orifice]
    S4 --> T2[External Mouthpiece]
    T1 --> R1[Record x and y for Trajectory]
    T1 --> R2[Record Collection Time and Rise]
    T2 --> R3[Record Collection Time and Rise]
    R1 --> CALC[Calculate Cv Cc Cd]
    R2 --> CALC
    R3 --> CALC
    CALC --> END[Tabulate Results and Compare with Standard Values]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define the three hydraulic coefficients of an orifice. State their typical values for a sharp-edged orifice.**

**Model Answer:**
The three hydraulic coefficients of an orifice are:

1. **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd):** The ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge.
$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theo}}$$
Typical value ≈ **0.62**

2. **Coefficient of Velocity (Cv):** The ratio of actual velocity at the vena contracta to theoretical velocity.
$$C_v = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{theo}}$$
Typical value ≈ **0.97**

3. **Coefficient of Contraction (Cc):** The ratio of the area of the jet at vena contracta to the area of the orifice.
$$C_c = \frac{A_{vena\ contracta}}{A}$$
Typical value ≈ **0.64**

These satisfy the relation **Cd = Cc × Cv**.

---

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2. What is the vena contracta? Why does it form, and at what distance from a sharp-edged orifice does it occur?**

**Model Answer:**
The **vena contracta** is the location in a fluid jet where the cross-sectional area is minimum and the streamlines become parallel. 

**Why it forms:** When fluid approaches a sharp-edged orifice, the streamlines cannot make an instantaneous sharp turn at the edge. They continue to curve inward for some distance past the orifice plane due to inertia, reaching minimum area at the vena contracta before becoming parallel.

**Location:** Approximately **0.5 times the orifice diameter** beyond the orifice plane for a sharp-edged orifice. The ratio of vena contracta area to orifice area equals Cc.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam - End Semester Assessment, Module Experiment 2]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define Coefficient of Discharge (Cd), Coefficient of Velocity (Cv), and Coefficient of Contraction (Cc). Derive the relationship **Cd = Cc × Cv** using the continuity equation and Bernoulli's theorem.

**(b)** [7 Marks] In an experiment to determine the hydraulic coefficients of a sharp-edged orifice of diameter 25 mm, the following observations were recorded:
- Constant head above orifice, H = 2.5 m
- Trajectory test: horizontal distance x = 0.85 m, vertical fall y = 0.18 m
- Actual discharge: 8.5 litres collected in 30 seconds
- Take g = 9.81 m/s²

Calculate Cv, Cd, and Cc. Comment on the results.

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definitions:**
- **Cd** = (Qactual)/(Qtheo)
- **Cv** = (Vactual)/(Vtheo)
- **Cc** = (Avena contracta)/(Aorifice)

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's equation** between free surface (1) and vena contracta (2):

$$H = \frac{V_{actual}^2}{2g} + h_L$$

**Step 3 — Actual velocity:**

$$V_{actual} = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$$

**Step 4 — Apply continuity** (Area at vena contracta = Cc × A):

$$Q_{actual} = A_{vc} \times V_{actual} = C_c \cdot A \cdot C_v\sqrt{2gH}$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical discharge:**

$$Q_{theo} = A \sqrt{2gH}$$

**Step 6 — Form ratio:**

$$\boxed{C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theo}} = \frac{C_c \cdot A \cdot C_v\sqrt{2gH}}{A\sqrt{2gH}} = C_c \cdot C_v}$$

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Given:**
- D = 25 mm = 0.025 m → A = (π/4)(0.025)² = 4.909 × 10⁻⁴ m²
- H = 2.5 m, x = 0.85 m, y = 0.18 m
- Qactual = (8.5 × 10⁻³)/30 = 2.833 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s

**Step 1 — Calculate Cv (trajectory method):**

$$C_v = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{yH}} = \frac{0.85}{2\sqrt{0.18 \times 2.5}} = \frac{0.85}{2\sqrt{0.45}} = \frac{0.85}{2 \times 0.6708} = \frac{0.85}{1.3416} = 0.6336$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Qtheo:**

$$Q_{theo} = A\sqrt{2gH} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.5}$$

$$\sqrt{49.05} = 7.0036 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Q_{theo} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 7.0036 = 3.438 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Cd:**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theo}} = \frac{2.833 \times 10^{-4}}{3.438 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0824$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #1:**
> Notice the unrealistically low Cd. This is because the collected volume is small. In KTU board exams, students often blindly calculate without checking physical plausibility. Always verify Cc < 1.0 after computing.

**Step 4 — Calculate Cc:**

$$C_c = \frac{C_d}{C_v} = \frac{0.0824}{0.6336} = 0.130$$

Since Cc < 1.0, the result is physically valid but unusually low. In a properly conducted KTU experiment, expect Cd ≈ 0.62 and Cc ≈ 0.64.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat sketch the **Borda's Trajectory Method** for experimentally determining the Coefficient of Velocity (Cv) of an orifice. State the formula used.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A cylindrical mouthpiece of diameter 30 mm is fitted to a tank having a constant head of 4 m. If the coefficient of discharge is 0.85, find:
(i) The actual discharge through the mouthpiece
(ii) The velocity of the jet at the vena contracta (inside the mouthpiece)
(iii) Compare the discharge with a sharp-edged orifice under the same head.

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Solution:**

**Borda's Trajectory Method** is used to determine Cv experimentally.

**Procedure:**
1. The tank is filled with water and maintained at a constant head **H** above the orifice by means of an overflow arrangement.
2. A jet issues horizontally from the orifice.
3. Two sharp pointers are arranged — one to measure the **horizontal distance x** and another to measure the **vertical fall y** of the jet.
4. The horizontal distance is measured from the vena contracta to the point where the jet strikes the vertical scale.
5. The vertical distance is measured from the orifice centreline to the point of strike.

**Derivation:** (As shown in Section 3.1)

Using projectile motion:
- Time of fall: $t = \sqrt{2y/g}$
- Horizontal velocity: $V = x/t = x\sqrt{g/2y}$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{C_v = \frac{V_{actual}}{\sqrt{2gH}} = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{yH}}}$$

**Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.2 schematic — a tank with overflow, sharp-edged orifice at depth H, jet tracing parabolic trajectory with marked x and y coordinates)

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Given:**
- D = 30 mm = 0.030 m → A = (π/4)(0.030)² = 7.069 × 10⁻⁴ m²
- H = 4 m, Cd = 0.85 (mouthpiece), g = 9.81 m/s²

**(i) Actual discharge through mouthpiece:**

$$Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 4} = \sqrt{78.48} = 8.859 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Q_{actual} = 0.85 \times 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.859$$

$$Q_{actual} = 5.323 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.323 \text{ L/s}$$

**(ii) Velocity at vena contracta:**

For a mouthpiece, Cc = 1.0 and Cv = Cd = 0.855 (theoretical value).

$$V_{actual} = C_v \sqrt{2gH} = 0.855 \times 8.859 = 7.574 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Comparison with sharp-edged orifice:**

For sharp-edged orifice, Cd = 0.62 (standard):

$$Q_{orifice} = 0.62 \times 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.859 = 3.882 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Percentage increase in discharge with mouthpiece:**

$$\% \text{ Increase} = \frac{Q_{mouthpiece} - Q_{orifice}}{Q_{orifice}} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{5.323 - 3.882}{3.882} \times 100 = \frac{1.441}{3.882} \times 100 = 37.12\%$$

**Conclusion:** A mouthpiece delivers approximately **37% more discharge** than a sharp-edged orifice under identical head conditions, demonstrating its engineering superiority for flow applications.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #2:**
> When asked to compare orifice and mouthpiece, students often write that mouthpiece Cd > orifice Cd but forget to compute the **actual percentage improvement**. KTU board examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the numerical comparison. Always compute the percentage and explain.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Equation (must memorize):** $C_d = C_c \times C_v$
- **Torricelli's Velocity:** $V_{theo} = \sqrt{2gH}$
- **Theoretical Discharge:** $Q_{theo} = A\sqrt{2gH}$
- **Cv formula (trajectory method):** $C_v = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{yH}}$
- **Vena contracta location for orifice:** ~0.5D outside the orifice
- **Vena contracta location for mouthpiece:** inside the tube
- **Pressure at vena contracta in mouthpiece:** **below atmospheric** (creates suction that boosts discharge)
- **Standard Cd values:** Orifice ≈ 0.62 | Mouthpiece ≈ 0.855
- **Standard Cc values:** Orifice ≈ 0.64 | Mouthpiece = 1.00
- **Standard Cv values:** Orifice ≈ 0.97 | Mouthpiece ≈ 0.855
- **Mouthpiece discharge is ~37% higher than orifice** under identical conditions
- **Experimental methods:** Volumetric (for Cd), Trajectory (for Cv), Jet diameter measurement (for Cc)
- **Constant head maintenance** is essential — use overflow or feed tank arrangement
- **Energy/Head loss relation:** $h_L = H(1 - C_v^2)$
- **Physical plausibility check:** Always verify 0 < Cd, Cc, Cv ≤ 1.0 in numerical problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Tip:** In the exam, if you are asked to derive **Cd = Cc × Cv**, ALWAYS start from the definitions, apply continuity, and finish with the ratio — examiners look for this three-step logical structure and award full marks only when all three steps are explicit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Triangular and Rectangular Notches
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL308 - CONTINUUM MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Triangular and Rectangular Notches

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **notch** is a device used for measuring the rate of flow of a liquid through a small tank or reservoir. It is essentially an opening made in the sidewall of a tank, shaped either as a **rectangle (Rectangular Notch)** or a **triangle (Triangular / V-Notch)**, through which the liquid flows out. Calibration refers to the experimental determination of the **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)** by comparing the *actual discharge* measured volumetrically with the *theoretical discharge* computed using the head over the notch crest.

**Triangular Notch (V-Notch):** A V-shaped opening with vertex angle $\theta$, generally preferred for measuring low and precise discharges because it offers a wider working range of head for a given flow range.

**Rectangular Notch:** A rectangular opening of width $L$, generally used when discharges are comparatively larger and the head available is small.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPEL308):**
> Notches are classified under the broader category of **open channel / free-flow measuring devices**. Unlike orifices (which are submerged), notches discharge freely into the atmosphere. The **Continuum Mechanics Lab** specifically demands the student calibrate both notch types, plot $Q_{actual}$ versus $Q_{theoretical}$ curves, and report $C_d$ as the slope of the best-fit line.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen washbasin** with a rectangular opening cut into its side near the bottom (this is your rectangular notch). Water fills the basin, and as the level rises, water pours out faster. Now imagine the same setup but with a **V-shaped cut** like a slice of cake removed from the side — the higher the water level, the wider the gap for water to escape (because the V opens up as you go up). 

The triangular notch is **more sensitive at low heads** (small flows) because its discharge varies as $H^{5/2}$ (a steep dependence on head), whereas the rectangular notch varies as $H^{3/2}$. For an analogy: if you whisper into a microphone, you want high sensitivity — that's the V-notch's role.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why calibrate?**
> The theoretical discharge equation assumes **ideal flow** (no friction, no contraction, perfect fluid). Real flow experiences:
> - **Vena contracta** formation (flow contracts below the notch).
> - **Surface tension** and viscous effects near the edges.
> - **Velocity of approach** (water is not stationary as it approaches the notch).
>
> These real-world losses are bundled into a single empirical constant: the **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)**, which we determine experimentally.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard)
- Typical $C_d$ range for a **clean sharp V-notch**: $C_d \approx 0.58$ to $0.62$
- Typical $C_d$ range for a **clean sharp rectangular notch**: $C_d \approx 0.61$ to $0.66$
- Standard head measurement using a **pointer gauge** (vernier-type) reading to **0.1 mm**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

For both notch types, the theoretical derivation follows the same four-step pattern:

1. **Consider an elemental horizontal strip** of liquid at depth $h$ below the free surface (or height $h$ above the crest), of infinitesimal thickness $dh$.
2. **Compute the area** $dA$ of the strip using the geometry of the notch.
3. **Apply Torricelli's Law** to find the theoretical velocity through the strip: $v_t = \sqrt{2gh}$.
4. **Integrate** the elemental discharge $dQ = C_d \cdot dA \cdot v_t$ from $h = 0$ to $h = H$ (head over the crest).

### 2.1.1 Triangular Notch (V-Notch) — Geometry

Consider a V-notch with total vertex angle $\theta$. At any height $h$ above the apex (vertex), the width of the flow cross-section $w$ is given by simple trigonometry:

$$w = 2h \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

The elemental area strip at depth $h$ is:

$$dA = w \, dh = 2 \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot h \, dh$$

Applying Torricelli's velocity and integrating from $0$ to $H$:

$$Q_{th} = \int_0^H C_d \cdot 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot h \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \, dh$$

Solving the integral:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{8}{15} \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$$

### 2.1.2 Rectangular Notch — Geometry

For a rectangular notch of width $L$, the elemental strip width is constant:

$$dA = L \, dh$$

$$Q_{th} = \int_0^H C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \, dh = \frac{2}{3} C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:**
> The exponent in $H$ is **2.5** for triangular notches vs **1.5** for rectangular notches. This is why triangular notches give *much* higher resolution (and are preferred) for small discharges, while rectangular notches are used for high discharges where a large $H$ would cause flooding of the upstream tank.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Notch Type | Theoretical Discharge $Q_{th}$ | Actual Discharge $Q_{act}$ | Coefficient $C_d$ | Typical $C_d$ Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Triangular (V)** | $\dfrac{8}{15} \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$ | $\dfrac{V}{t}$ (volumetric) | $\dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | $0.58 - 0.62$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | **Rectangular** | $\dfrac{2}{3} L \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$ | $\dfrac{V}{t}$ (volumetric) | $\dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | $0.61 - 0.66$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 3 | **Rehbock formula** (rect., reference) | $\left(0.405 + \dfrac{0.0027}{H}\right) L \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$ | — | Built-in | $H, L$ in m | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | **Francis formula** (rect., w/ end contractions) | $1.84 \, (L - 0.2H) \, H^{3/2}$ | — | Built-in (FPS units) | — | $\text{ft}^3/\text{s}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Pitfall:** In the $Q_{th}$ formula, the term $\sqrt{2g}$ is dimensionless *only if* $g$ is in $\text{m/s}^2$ and $H$ is in meters. The exponent on $H$ automatically yields the correct discharge unit: $\text{m}^{5/2} \cdot \text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s} = \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. **Always verify dimensional consistency before substitution.**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sewage Treatment Plants:** V-notches are used as effluent flow meters in weir boxes.
- **Hydropower Canals:** Rectangular notches measure flow in irrigation canals and small hydroelectric intake channels.
- **Cooling Water Systems:** Industrial cooling towers use rectangular notches for chemical dosing calibration.
- **Dam Spillways:** Large-scale rectangular weirs (essentially notches) measure river discharge for flood forecasting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Triangular Notch Discharge

### Step 1 — Geometry Setup
Let the vertex angle of the V-notch be $\theta$. Consider the apex as the origin. At a height $h$ above the apex, draw a horizontal elemental strip of thickness $dh$. The strip lies across the V-opening.

By symmetry, each side of the V makes an angle $\theta/2$ with the vertical centerline. The half-width at height $h$ is:

$$\frac{w}{2} = h \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

Therefore the total width of the strip is:

$$w = 2h \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Elemental Area
$$dA = w \, dh = 2h \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \, dh$$

### Step 3 — Theoretical Velocity (Torricelli's Law)
The water surface is at height $H$ above the apex. The strip is at height $h$, so the *height of water above the strip* is $(H - h)$.

$$v_t = \sqrt{2g(H - h)}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Torricelli's law follows from Bernoulli's equation between the free surface (velocity $\approx 0$, pressure = atmospheric) and the strip exit (velocity $v_t$, pressure = atmospheric). Energy per unit weight: $H - h = v_t^2 / 2g \Rightarrow v_t = \sqrt{2g(H-h)}$.

### Step 4 — Elemental Discharge (Ideal)
$$dQ_{ideal} = v_t \, dA = \sqrt{2g(H-h)} \cdot 2h \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \, dh$$

### Step 5 — Apply Empirical Coefficient
$$dQ_{actual} = C_d \, dQ_{ideal} = 2 C_d \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, h \sqrt{H - h} \, dh$$

### Step 6 — Integrate from $h = 0$ to $h = H$
$$Q_{act} = 2 C_d \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H h \sqrt{H - h} \, dh$$

**Evaluating the integral** using the substitution $u = H - h$, $du = -dh$, so $h = H - u$:

$$\int_0^H h \sqrt{H-h} \, dh = \int_0^H (H - u) \sqrt{u} \, du$$

$$= H \int_0^H u^{1/2} du - \int_0^H u^{3/2} du$$

$$= H \cdot \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2} - \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2}$$

$$= \frac{2}{3} H^{5/2} - \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2}$$

$$= \frac{10 - 6}{15} H^{5/2} = \frac{4}{15} H^{5/2}$$

### Step 7 — Substitute Back
$$Q_{act} = 2 C_d \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{4}{15} H^{5/2}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the canonical **King's formula** for V-notch weirs (named after Horace King, 1894). It is universally cited in KTU examination answer keys.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Rectangular Notch Discharge

### Step 1 — Geometry Setup
Width of the notch = $L$ (constant). Consider an elemental horizontal strip at depth $(H - h)$ below the free surface, of thickness $dh$ and area $dA = L \, dh$.

### Step 2 — Theoretical Velocity
$$v_t = \sqrt{2g(H - h)}$$

### Step 3 — Elemental Discharge
$$dQ_{ideal} = v_t \, dA = L \sqrt{2g(H - h)} \, dh$$

### Step 4 — Integrate
$$Q_{ideal} = L \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H \sqrt{H - h} \, dh$$

Let $u = H - h$, $du = -dh$:

$$\int_0^H \sqrt{H-h} \, dh = \int_0^H u^{1/2} du = \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2}$$

### Step 5 — Apply $C_d$
$$\boxed{Q_{act} = \frac{2}{3} C_d \, L \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}}$$

## 3.3 Calibration Procedure — Analytical Matrix

| Step | Action | Instrument / Equation | Observation Recorded |
|------|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Set up the notch plate in the experimental tank | Sump tank, pump, notch plate, pointer gauge | Geometry ($\theta$ or $L$) |
| 2 | Allow water to fill the tank and overflow through the notch | Pump at constant discharge | Steady-state condition |
| 3 | Measure the head $H$ above the notch crest | **Hook gauge / Pointer gauge** | $H$ in meters |
| 4 | Collect outflow in a measuring tank for time $t$ | Measuring tank, stopwatch | Volume $V$, time $t$ |
| 5 | Compute $Q_{act} = V/t$ | Arithmetic | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 6 | Compute $Q_{th}$ using the formula with $C_d = 1$ | Calculator | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 7 | Compute $C_d = Q_{act} / Q_{th}$ | Arithmetic | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Repeat for **at least 5 different heads** | Vary pump discharge | Tabulated $C_d$ |
| 9 | Plot $Q_{act}$ (y-axis) vs $Q_{th}$ (x-axis) | Graph paper | Slope = mean $C_d$ |
| 10 | Plot $C_d$ vs $H$ to verify constancy | Graph paper | Validates calibration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Graphical Method Validation:**
> For a properly calibrated setup, the plot of $Q_{act}$ vs $Q_{th}$ should yield a **straight line passing through the origin**, with slope equal to the **mean $C_d$**. The plot of $C_d$ versus $H$ should be **approximately horizontal**, confirming that $C_d$ is independent of head (within experimental error).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 60° V-notch is used to measure flow. The head over the apex is $H = 0.20$ m. The actual discharge collected is $V = 35$ liters in $t = 25$ seconds. Determine the coefficient of discharge. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Solution:**

$$Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{35 \times 10^{-3}}{25} = 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Theoretical discharge (with $C_d = 1$):

$$Q_{th} = \frac{8}{15} \tan\!\left(\frac{60°}{2}\right) \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \cdot (0.20)^{5/2}$$

Computing each part:

$$\tan(30°) = 0.5774$$

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294$$

$$(0.20)^{5/2} = (0.20)^{2.5} = 0.20^2 \cdot \sqrt{0.20} = 0.04 \cdot 0.4472 = 0.01789$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{8}{15} \times 0.5774 \times 4.4294 \times 0.01789$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.5333 \times 0.5774 \times 4.4294 \times 0.01789$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.02440 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.0014}{0.02440} = 0.0574$$

> **Note to student:** This unusually low value (0.057) indicates either an experimental error, a clogged notch, or the head was measured incorrectly. A properly functioning 60° V-notch should yield $C_d \approx 0.58-0.62$. **Re-check the head measurement.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sumpA[Sump Tank] --> pumpA[Centrifugal Pump]
    pumpA --> valveA[Flow Control Valve]
    valveA --> stillingA[Stilling Chamber]
    stillingA --> testTankA[Test Section Tank]
    
    notchPlateA[Notch Plate Mounted Vertically] --> testTankA
    
    pointerG[A[Pointer Hook Gauge]] --> testTankA
    pointerG --> pointerReadA[Head H Reading]
    
    testTankA --> outflowA[Discharge through Notch]
    outflowA --> measTankA[Volumetric Measuring Tank]
    measTankA --> stopwatchA[Stopwatch for Time t]
    
    pointerReadA --> calcA[Compute Qth and Qact]
    stopwatchA --> calcA
    calcA --> cdA[Determine Cd]
    cdA --> plotA[Plot Qact vs Qth and Cd vs H]
    
    sumpA -.return line.-> sumpA
```

## 4.2 Triangular Notch Flow Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    freeSurfA[Free Water Surface] --> headA[Head H above apex]
    headA --> apexA[Apex of V-Notch]
    
    apexA --> leftSideA[Left edge at angle theta/2]
    apexA --> rightSideA[Right edge at angle theta/2]
    
    stripA[Elemental strip at height h] --> apexA
    
    widthA[w = 2h tan theta/2] --> stripA
    vA[vt = sqrt 2g H minus h] --> stripA
    dQA[dQ = Cd w dh vt] --> stripA
    stripA --> intA[Integrate h: 0 to H]
    intA --> finalA[Q = 8/15 Cd tan theta/2 sqrt 2g H to power 5/2]
```

## 4.3 Rectangular Notch Flow Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    freeSurfB[Free Water Surface] --> headB[Head H above crest]
    headB --> crestB[Rectangular Notch Crest]
    
    crestB --> leftWallB[Left vertical edge]
    crestB --> rightWallB[Right vertical edge]
    
    widthLB[L = constant width] --> stripB[Elemental strip L times dh]
    stripB --> vB[vt = sqrt 2g H minus h]
    vB --> dQB[dQ = Cd L sqrt 2g H minus h dh]
    dQB --> intB[Integrate h: 0 to H]
    intB --> finalB[Q = 2/3 Cd L sqrt 2g H to power 3/2]
```

## 4.4 Calibration Plot Interpretation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA[Start: Tabulate H, t, V] --> qactA[Compute Qact = V/t]
    qactA --> qthA[Compute Qth with Cd = 1]
    qthA --> cdInstA[Compute Cd inst = Qact / Qth]
    cdInstA --> cdMeanA[Compute mean Cd]
    cdMeanA --> plotQA[Plot Qact Y axis vs Qth X axis]
    plotQA --> slopeCheckA{Slope equals mean Cd?}
    slopeCheckA -->|Yes| validA[Calibration Valid]
    slopeCheckA -->|No| errorA[Check for systematic error]
    
    cdInstA --> plotH[Plot Cd vs H]
    plotH --> flatCheck{Approximately horizontal?}
    flatCheck -->|Yes| validA
    flatCheck -->|No| errorA
```
