## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
**Define metacentric height. What does a positive metacentric height indicate about a floating body?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 Marks):** Metacentric height is defined as the distance between the center of gravity ($G$) of a floating body and the metacenter ($M$). It is mathematically expressed as $GM = KM - KG$, where $K$ is the keel point and $M$ is the point where the line of action of the new buoyant force (after a small tilt) intersects the original vertical centerline of the body.

> **Significance (1 Mark):** A **positive metacentric height ($GM > 0$)** indicates that the floating body is in **stable equilibrium**. This means if the body is slightly tilted from its equilibrium position, it will experience a righting (restoring) couple that brings it back to its original upright position. The body will not capsize for small disturbances.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
**State the formula used to determine metacentric height experimentally using the plumb line method and explain each term.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula (1.5 Marks):** $GM = \dfrac{W \cdot d \cdot a}{w \cdot x}$
>
> **Explanation of Terms (1.5 Marks):**
> - $W$ = Weight of the movable (shifter) mass in Newtons
> - $d$ = Horizontal distance through which the shifter is moved from the center in meters
> - $a$ = Vertical length of the plumb line (from suspension point to waterline reference) in meters
> - $w$ = Total weight of the floating body (pontoon + any fixed loads) in Newtons
> - $x$ = Horizontal deflection of the plumb bob after the body tilts in meters

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Derive the formula for determining the metacentric height of a floating body using the **tilt angle method**. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular pontoon of length **$1.5$ m**, breadth **$1.0$ m**, and weighing **$300$ N** floats in water. A weight of **$20$ N** is placed on the pontoon. If the pontoon is tilted through an angle of **$5°$** by shifting the weight by **$0.4$ m** from the center, calculate the metacentric height. Comment on the stability of the pontoon. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Initial Setup (1 Mark):**

Consider a floating body (pontoon) of total weight $w$ floating in equilibrium. A small additional weight $W$ is placed on the body at its geometric center.

**Step 2 — Apply the Shifter (1 Mark):**

The weight $W$ is shifted horizontally by a distance $d$ from the centerline. This creates a **heeling (tilting) moment** about the original centerline:

$$M_{heeling} = W \cdot d$$

**Step 3 — Body Tilt and Restoring Moment (1 Mark):**

The body tilts by a small angle $\theta$ until equilibrium is reached. The new center of buoyancy shifts, creating a **restoring (righting) moment**:

$$M_{righting} = w \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium Condition (1 Mark):**

At the new equilibrium position, heeling moment = righting moment:

$$W \cdot d = w \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 5 — Solve for GM (1 Mark):**

$$GM = \frac{W \cdot d}{w \cdot \sin\theta}$$

**Step 6 — Small-Angle Approximation (1 Mark):**

For small tilt angles ($\theta < 10°$), the small-angle approximation applies:

$$\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta \text{ (in radians)}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{GM = \frac{W \cdot d}{w \cdot \tan\theta}}$$

**Step 7 — Assumptions (1 Mark):**

The key assumptions are:
1. The tilt angle $\theta$ is small (less than ~$10°$).
2. The metacenter $M$ remains fixed during tilting.
3. The weight $w$ of the body does not change (no water entry/exit).
4. The center of gravity $G$ is fixed relative to the body.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $L = 1.5$ m (length)
- $B = 1.0$ m (breadth)
- $w_p = 300$ N (pontoon weight)
- $W = 20$ N (shifter weight)
- $\theta = 5°$ (tilt angle)
- $d = 0.4$ m (shifter distance)

**Step 1 — Total Weight of the System (1 Mark):**

$$w = w_p + W = 300 + 20 = 320 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Tilt Angle to Tangent (1 Mark):**

$$\tan 5° = 0.0875$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Tilt Angle Formula (2 Marks):**

$$GM = \frac{W \cdot d}{w \cdot \tan\theta}$$

$$GM = \frac{20 \times 0.4}{320 \times 0.0875}$$

$$GM = \frac{8.0}{28.0}$$

$$GM = 0.2857 \text{ m} \approx 28.57 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Metacentric Radius $BM$ (1 Mark):**

Submerged depth of pontoon (assuming water density = $1000$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s²):

$$V_{sub} = \frac{w}{\rho_{water} \cdot g} = \frac{300}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.0306 \text{ m}^3$$

$$h = \frac{V_{sub}}{L \times B} = \frac{0.0306}{1.5 \times 1.0} = 0.0204 \text{ m}$$

$$BM = \frac{B^2}{12h} = \frac{(1.0)^2}{12 \times 0.0204} = 4.085 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Determine $KG$ (1 Mark):**

$$KG = BM - GM = 4.085 - 0.2857 = 3.799 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Stability Comment (1 Mark):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $GM = 0.2857$ m $> 0$, the pontoon is **STABLE**. The relatively large $BM$ value (4.085 m) compared to $GM$ (0.2857 m) indicates that the center of gravity is significantly above the center of buoyancy, which is typical for vessels carrying heavy deck loads.

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the experimental procedure to determine the metacentric height of a floating body using the **plumb line method**. List the observations and calculations required. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A floating pontoon weighing **$50$ N** (empty) carries a movable weight of **$5$ N**. When the weight is shifted by **$10$ cm**, the plumb line of length **$30$ cm** deflects by **$2$ cm**. Calculate the metacentric height. If the breadth of the pontoon is **$20$ cm** and submerged depth is **$5$ cm**, verify whether the result is consistent with theoretical prediction. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Procedure Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Setup (1 Mark):**

Fill the water tank to a suitable level. Place the rectangular floating pontoon gently on the water surface. Ensure the pontoon floats freely and is not touching the tank walls.

**Step 2 — Add the Shifter at Center (1 Mark):**

Place the movable weight (shifter) of known mass $W$ at the geometric center of the pontoon. Wait for complete equilibrium (pontoon becomes stationary and water settles).

**Step 3 — Suspend Plumb Line (1 Mark):**

Suspend a plumb line from a fixed point on the pontoon (typically from a hook above the deck) such that the bob hangs freely just above a graduated vertical scale. Record the initial reading of the plumb bob position $x_0$.

**Step 4 — Measure Plumb Length (1 Mark):**

Measure and record the vertical distance $a$ from the suspension point to the reference scale (effectively, the length of the plumb line above the waterline).

**Step 5 — Shift the Weight (1 Mark):**

Carefully shift the movable weight $W$ by a measured distance $d$ (e.g., 5 cm, 10 cm, 15 cm) along the graduated scale on the deck of the pontoon. Wait for the pontoon to reach new equilibrium.

**Step 6 — Record New Position (1 Mark):**

Record the new position of the plumb bob $x_1$. Calculate the horizontal deflection $x = x_1 - x_0$.

**Step 7 — Repeat and Tabulate (1 Mark):**

Repeat the experiment for at least 5 different values of $d$ (5 cm, 10 cm, 15 cm, 20 cm, 25 cm). Tabulate the readings and calculate $GM$ for each trial using the formula $GM = \dfrac{W \cdot d \cdot a}{w \cdot x}$. Compute the average value of $GM$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $w_p = 50$ N (pontoon weight)
- $W = 5$ N (shifter weight)
- $d = 10$ cm $= 0.10$ m
- $a = 30$ cm $= 0.30$ m
- $x = 2$ cm $= 0.02$ m
- $B = 20$ cm $= 0.20$ m
- $h = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m

**Step 1 — Total Weight (1 Mark):**

$$w = w_p + W = 50 + 5 = 55 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Plumb Line Formula (2 Marks):**

$$GM = \frac{W \cdot d \cdot a}{w \cdot x}$$

$$GM = \frac{5 \times 0.10 \times 0.30}{55 \times 0.02}$$

$$GM = \frac{0.15}{1.10}$$

$$GM = 0.1364 \text{ m} = 13.64 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Theoretical $BM$ (1 Mark):**

$$BM_{theoretical} = \frac{B^2}{12h} = \frac{(0.20)^2}{12 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.04}{0.60} = 0.0667 \text{ m} = 6.67 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4 — Determine Theoretical $KG$ (1 Mark):**

For a uniform rectangular pontoon, the center of gravity lies at the geometric center of the submerged portion (approximately):

$$KG = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{0.05}{2} = 0.025 \text{ m} = 2.5 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Theoretical $GM$ (1 Mark):**

$$GM_{theoretical} = BM - KG = 0.0667 - 0.025 = 0.0417 \text{ m} = 4.17 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 6 — Consistency Check (1 Mark):**

| Quantity | Experimental | Theoretical | Difference |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $GM$ | 13.64 cm | 4.17 cm | 9.47 cm (overestimate) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The experimental value is **larger** than the theoretical value. This is because in the experimental setup, the movable weight $W$ is placed **on top** of the pontoon, raising the actual center of gravity $G$ much higher than the theoretical $h/2$ value (which assumes $G$ at the center of the submerged block). The experimental result is **physically consistent** with the actual geometry of the system. The error is systematic (not random) and can be reduced by accounting for the exact position of the center of gravity including the shifter.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (Major Pitfall):** Students often mix units — for example, using $W$ in kg and $d$ in cm without converting to consistent SI units (N and m). **Always convert all quantities to SI (Newtons and meters) before substituting into the formula.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Small-Angle Approximation:** Using $\sin\theta$ instead of $\tan\theta$ (or vice versa) without justification. For angles less than $10°$, both give nearly the same answer, but examiners expect you to explicitly state the small-angle assumption.
>
> 3. **Neglecting the Shifter's Own Weight in $w$:** The total weight $w$ must include the shifter's weight. If the pontoon weight is $w_p$ and shifter is $W$, then $w = w_p + W$.
>
> 4. **Confusing $BM$ with $GM$:** $BM$ is the metacentric RADIUS (from buoyancy center to metacenter). $GM$ is the metacentric HEIGHT (from gravity center to metacenter). They are NOT the same.
>
> 5. **Sign Error in Stability Assessment:** A negative $GM$ does NOT mean "no stability" — it explicitly means UNSTABLE (the body will capsize). Students often write "marginally stable" instead of "unstable."
>
> 6. **Omitting the Plumb Line Length $a$:** In the plumb line method, forgetting to multiply by $a$ is a very common 1-mark deduction. The formula has FOUR factors in the numerator.
>
> 7. **No Discussion of Experimental Error:** KTU examiners reward students who identify sources of error (e.g., parallax in plumb line reading, water surface ripples, wall friction, weight placement imprecision).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Metacentric Height Determination**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ **Metacentric Height ($GM$)** = Distance from Center of Gravity ($G$) to Metacenter ($M$)
- ☐ **Metacenter ($M$)** = Intersection point of new buoyant force line with original vertical centerline after a small tilt
- ☐ **Center of Buoyancy ($B$)** = Centroid of displaced fluid volume
- ☐ **Metacentric Radius ($BM$)** = Distance from $B$ to $M$ = $I / V_{sub}$

**Key Formulas (MUST memorize):**
- ☐ $GM = \dfrac{W \cdot d}{w \cdot \tan\theta}$ (Tilt angle method)
- ☐ $GM = \dfrac{W \cdot d \cdot a}{w \cdot x}$ (Plumb line method)
- ☐ $GM = BM - KG$ (Fundamental relationship)
- ☐ $BM = \dfrac{B^2}{12h}$ (For rectangular pontoon)

**Stability Criterion:**
- ☐ $GM > 0$ → **STABLE** (Self-righting)
- ☐ $GM = 0$ → **NEUTRAL** (No restoring force)
- ☐ $GM < 0$ → **UNSTABLE** (Will capsize)

**Experimental Procedure Points:**
- ☐ Take 5 readings for different values of $d$
- ☐ Average the $GM$ values for the final answer
- ☐ Compare with theoretical $BM - KG$ value
- ☐ Calculate percentage error

**Common Error Sources to Mention:**
- ☐ Parallax error in plumb line reading
- ☐ Water surface ripples and waves
- ☐ Pontoon touching tank walls
- ☐ Inaccurate weight placement
- ☐ Friction at the support point
- ☐ Approximation of small angle

**Engineering Applications:**
- ☐ Ship design (naval architecture)
- ☐ Submarine ballast control
- ☐ Offshore platform stability
- ☐ Floating bridges and pontoons
- ☐ Rocket fuel slosh dynamics

**Quick Mental Math:**
- ☐ For $\theta = 5°$, $\tan\theta = 0.0875$ (remember this value!)
- ☐ For $\theta = 10°$, $\tan\theta = 0.1763$
- ☐ For $\theta = 15°$, $\tan\theta = 0.2679$
- ☐ Density of water = $1000$ kg/m³ (or $\rho g = 9810$ N/m³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU Exam:** Always draw a clear, labeled sketch of the floating body showing the positions of $G$, $B$, $M$, and $K$ in BOTH the initial and tilted positions. This simple act alone can earn 1-2 extra marks in the valuation, even if your numerical answer has a small error.
